Stream: General

Topic: Spinlocks with IRQs?

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 19:49):

Hi! Has anyone worked on adding spinlocks that save/restore IRQs when being locked/unlocked? I have a small patch to add it that I wouldn't mind trying to upstream, but I figured I should ask around to make sure no one else has gotten to this yet

Alice Ryhl (Jul 23 2024 at 19:51):

What are you using it for?

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 19:54):

Alice Ryhl said:

What are you using it for?

RVKMS (the rust port of the VKMS kernel driver), specifically for working with vertical blanking where DRM's expectation is that most vertical blanks come from an actual interrupt - meaning all of the vblank related spinlocks in KMS disable/enable spinlocks by default
(to be honest I was a bit surprised it wasn't added already, I see far more spinlocks being grabbed under interrupts then ones not being grabbed under interrupts)

Alice Ryhl (Jul 23 2024 at 19:54):

I don't think anyone has put one together yet.

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 19:55):

ah cool, honestly it's simple enough I might just send the patch onto the mailing list today or tomorrow.

Boqun Feng (Jul 23 2024 at 20:11):

Be aware of this

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 20:17):

oh dear haha, that might explain why this hasn't been upstreamed yet

Boqun Feng (Jul 23 2024 at 20:20):

Either we make it unsafe or we say we rely on klint to avoid the bad case.

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 20:22):

that seems kind of painful tbh, I'd kinda rather not make something this core to a lot of drivers need to be unsafe.
Why not have it so that we treat IRQ state as a separate object that disables interrupts once you get the object - and then you have to pass that state object to lock()/unlock() whenever you want to grab an IRQ that's known to be grabbed with interrupts disabled?

Boqun Feng (Jul 23 2024 at 20:25):

You will have a similar issue when you have two local_irq_save(), unless you track the nest level of those in a per-thread or per-cpu variable, right?

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 20:27):

I'm curious why we'd need to track the nesting level? If you disable IRQs once and then try to save the IRQ disable flag again, isn't the second set of saved flags going to just be a no-op when we restore them anyway?

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 20:27):

We would need to at the very least make sure that whatever guard we return from the spinlock borrows from the actual IRQ guard

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 20:29):

or hm, I guess that doesn't stop someone from just dropping the irq guards in the wrong order

Boqun Feng (Jul 23 2024 at 20:29):

Say:

// IRQ is enabled
let guard1 = irq_save();
// IRQ is disabled.
let guard2 = irq_save();
drop(guard1);
// IRQ is enabled while guard2 exists.

Boqun Feng (Jul 23 2024 at 20:30):

Lyude said:

or hm, I guess that doesn't stop someone from just dropping the irq guards in the wrong order

Yeah..

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 20:31):

this is definitely a common enough usecase though I'm really hesistant to just rely on unsafe unless we know there's a better solution on the horizon

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 20:33):

oh wow someone just suggested a good idea: what about using closures for this

Boqun Feng (Jul 23 2024 at 20:34):

Maybe a closure API, like:

fn with_irq_save<F: FnOnce()>(f: F) {
    let mut flags;
    unsafe { local_irq_save(&flags); }
    f();
    unsafe { local_irq_restore(&flags); }
}

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 20:34):

oh hah, looks like we both arrived at the same idea :P

Boqun Feng (Jul 23 2024 at 20:34):

"hey, that's my idea"

Boqun Feng (Jul 23 2024 at 20:34):

Lyude said:

oh hah, looks like we both arrived at the same idea :P

;-)

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 20:36):

anyway yeah - this seems like a good idea to me

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 20:52):

...there is one other idea that just got suggested to me. I'm fine with closures but this idea seems neat enough it's worth mentioning:
what if we had a global IrqState ZST type that you have to take mutable borrows from in order to modify the state of IRQs, and we just wrote a safe wrapper around it?

Boqun Feng (Jul 23 2024 at 20:56):

global or percpu?

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 20:56):

presumably this would possibly result in multiple &mut in different location, but considering it's ZST and doesn't

Boqun Feng said:

global or percpu?

Agh! good point, it would have to be per-cpu but then I guess I have no idea if that's possible

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 20:57):

oh - apparently statics can't be borrowed mutably anyway so nvm :)

Lyude (Jul 23 2024 at 20:57):

closures it is then!

Trevor Gross (Jul 23 2024 at 23:46):

I'm not sure how exactly it parallels, but this sort of pattern is typical in embedded rust https://docs.rs/critical-section/latest/critical_section/

Lyude (Jul 24 2024 at 17:54):

@Boqun Feng JFYI, wrote something up last night and this morning that I think does the trick, though I still need to split it into separate commits and finish up writing documentation/examples of course::
https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/blob/rvkms-wip/rust/kernel/irq.rs?ref_type=heads ← Added a IrqGuard<'a> type which is basically just an unsafe promise that interrupts are disabled, along with disable_irqs() which actually disable interrupts and provides access to an IrqGuard to the closure
https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/blob/rvkms-wip/rust/kernel/sync/lock/spinlock.rs?ref_type=heads#L142 ← Added an IrqSpinLock type that is basically just a container around a normal SpinLock except that it requires a reference to an IrqGuard
https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/blob/rvkms-wip/rust/kernel/sync/lock.rs?ref_type=heads#L200 ← and finally, added a LockContainer trait so that types like IrqSpinLock can be used in LockedBy in addition to normal lock types

Boqun Feng (Jul 24 2024 at 21:36):

LGTM, although I don't think *Guard is a good name, Guard usually means "dropping = state changing", I will start with IrqToken or IrqDisabled and see how others feel about it.

Lyude (Jul 24 2024 at 21:36):

sgtm

Trevor Gross (Jul 24 2024 at 22:04):

Maybe disable_irqs should be something with with to indicate it's a context, not a switch to disable IRQs. with_irq_disabled?

Lyude (Jul 24 2024 at 22:05):

ah yes - I actually renamed it to with_irqs_disabled in my local branch a bit ago because someone else pointed out that's part of the rust naming standards

Lyude (Jul 25 2024 at 21:28):

I guess I'll just get to submitting this upstream then? (Got stuff cleaned up and I'm pretty happy with it)

Boqun Feng (Jul 25 2024 at 21:28):

Sure, go ahead.

Lyude (Jul 25 2024 at 21:29):

I assume it's just rust-for-linux@vger.kernel.org ?

Boqun Feng (Jul 25 2024 at 21:30):

and linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org, and lock maintainers.

Boqun Feng (Jul 25 2024 at 21:31):

Looking for "LOCKING PRIMITIVES" in MAINTAINERS

Lyude (Jul 26 2024 at 17:59):

@Benno Lossin A question, since I get most of the fundamentals behind it but am still familiarizing myself with some of the terminology regarding SAFETY documentation. In response to the IrqDisabled type you mentioned:
"Another thing, I am wondering if we want this to be invariant over the lifetime, I don't have a good reason, but I still think we should consider it."
When you say "invariant over the lifetime", do you mean that we should specify that IRQs can never be enabled while a IrqDisabled exists in the current context?

Boqun Feng (Jul 26 2024 at 18:00):

No, I think Benno meant invariant of subtyping: https://doc.rust-lang.org/nomicon/subtyping.html

Boqun Feng (Jul 26 2024 at 18:01):

(welcome to category theory)

Lyude (Jul 26 2024 at 18:02):

hehe, I guess it's about time I sat down and learned. I've been winging it until now :P

Boqun Feng (Jul 26 2024 at 18:03):

basically, "invariant over the lifetime" means IrqDisabled<'a> is not a subtype of IrqDisabled<'b> even if 'a : 'b.

Lyude (Jul 26 2024 at 18:03):

Interesting

Lyude (Jul 26 2024 at 18:25):

Do we have any sort of kunit infrastructure for rust that I could use to write a test for this btw? I get the feeling Greg may want something that they can compile and run (though I might be wrong, they haven't responded to me yet) and having unit tests for something like this doesn't seem like a bad idea anyhow

Boqun Feng (Jul 26 2024 at 18:30):

Lyude said:

Do we have any sort of kunit infrastructure for rust that I could use to write a test for this btw? I get the feeling Greg may want something that they can compile and run (though I might be wrong, they haven't responded to me yet) and having unit tests for something like this doesn't seem like a bad idea anyhow

For now the code snippet in the comments will be tested by kunit, for example, the code before:

pub type SpinLock<T> = super::Lock<T, SpinLockBackend>;

in rust/kernel/sync/lock/spinlock.rs

Lyude (Jul 26 2024 at 18:31):

Cool - so all of the example code in rustdocs that I wrote should actually get tested?

Boqun Feng (Jul 26 2024 at 18:33):

Yes, it should be. Just run:

./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --make_options LLVM=1 --arch x86_64 --kconfig_add CONFIG_RUST=y

Boqun Feng (Jul 26 2024 at 18:34):

probably add --kconfig_add CONFIG_SMP=y as well, since by default smp is not enabled, and we probably care more about the smp case.

Boqun Feng (Jul 26 2024 at 18:34):

Boqun Feng said:

Yes, it should be. Just run:

./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --make_options LLVM=1 --arch x86_64 --kconfig_add CONFIG_RUST=y

BTW, this could be found at Documentation/rust/testing.rst

Miguel Ojeda (Jul 26 2024 at 18:45):

We will also have #[test] support (they will become KUnit tests, rather than what they are now) -- there is a series implementing it already.

Miguel Ojeda (Jul 26 2024 at 18:47):

If something makes sense as an example (i.e. doctests), it should be though!

Lyude (Jul 26 2024 at 18:47):

Boqun Feng said:

Boqun Feng said:

Yes, it should be. Just run:

./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --make_options LLVM=1 --arch x86_64 --kconfig_add CONFIG_RUST=y

BTW, this could be found at Documentation/rust/testing.rst

gotcha, I keep getting excited with this work and not reading docs clearly enough :P.

Boqun Feng (Jul 26 2024 at 19:08):

No worries. But now you’re obligated to tell three more people about this ;-)

Notification Bot (Jul 29 2024 at 15:50):

7 messages were moved from this topic to #General > KernelCI / Test Robot by Trevor Gross.

Lyude (Jul 30 2024 at 21:13):

@Boqun Feng so I think I'm starting to wrap my head around variance. I assume declaring IrqDisabled like this makes it invariant over lifetime 'a:

pub struct IrqDisabled<'a>(PhantomData<*mut &'a ()>);

correct?

Benno Lossin (Jul 30 2024 at 21:15):

that should be correct, but normally you would use PhantomData<fn(&'a ()) -> &'a ()>, since that would still be Send (though in our case I guess we would like it to not be).

Lyude (Jul 30 2024 at 21:17):

hm, I guess I'm not sure if we'd really want this? At least if I'm understanding correctly, we probably wouldn't want it to be invariant over the lifetime considering that we're already expecting interrupts to be disabled over the lifetime of IrqDisabled - and the only safe way of creating one (with_irqs_disabled()) enforces that through virtue of being a closure interface

Benno Lossin (Jul 30 2024 at 21:18):

at first I only stated the question without actually having any preference of invariant/covariant, but now I think I have an argument in favor of invariance:

Benno Lossin (Jul 30 2024 at 21:29):

scratch that, I just tested my hypothesis and turns out I was wrong :sweat_smile:

what I thought would be problematic was this:

    pub fn foo(&self, _: IrqDisabled<'_>) -> &'_ usize {
        &self.0
    }

I thought it would desugar to this:

    pub fn foo<'a>(&'a self, _: IrqDisabled<'a>) -> &'a usize {
        &self.0
    }

but it actually desugars to this:

    pub fn foo<'a, 'b>(&'a self, _: IrqDisabled<'b>) -> &'a usize {
        &self.0
    }

Lyude (Jul 30 2024 at 21:32):

Gotcha, so I guess I'll just keep it covariant for the time being (which to me makes sense, since we definitely want to be able to hand down IrqDisabled to other functions so they don't need to redundantly save/restore IRQ flags.

Benno Lossin (Jul 30 2024 at 21:33):

it being invariant doesn't prevent you from doing that though

Lyude (Jul 30 2024 at 21:34):

ah gotcha. yeah I'm still wrapping my head around this a bit loll

Benno Lossin (Jul 30 2024 at 21:34):

yeah no worries

Lyude (Jul 30 2024 at 21:35):

wait - is it being invariant in this case just saying that it needs its own lifetime?

Lyude (Jul 30 2024 at 21:36):

like - it can't be 'a if &self is &'a self

Benno Lossin (Jul 30 2024 at 21:36):

no not necessarily

Benno Lossin (Jul 30 2024 at 21:36):

being invariant means that you can assign IrqDisabled<'a> to IrqDisabled<'b> if and only if 'a == 'b

Benno Lossin (Jul 30 2024 at 21:37):

if IrqDisabled is covariant, you can assign IrqDisabled<'a> to IrqDisabled<'b> if and only if 'a: 'b, so if 'a outlives 'b.

Benno Lossin (Jul 30 2024 at 21:40):

but I guess one could expect that IrqDisabled is covariant, since it is a token that signifies that IRQs are disabled. you are allowed to shorten the lifetime on that, since IRQs will at least be disabled until it is dropped.

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:37):

I'm popping into this conversation uninvited, but because I need spin_lock_irqsave working quickly for a project I've essentially been doing parallel work to what Lyude's doing starting with the patchset they submitted on July 26th, and applying suggestions that Benno Lossin made to better structure Lock backends to add contexts.
There's one thing I noticed that might have not been discussed, and i apologize if it's already been mentioned i just couldn't find it: if lock_with requires a context by value, it might also be necessary to retrieve it upon unlock.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 15:38):

why do you think that unlock needs the context? in the case of IRQ, we can just make the guard live at most as long as the IrqDisabled token and everything should be fine.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 15:38):

do you have a different application?

Boqun Feng (Jul 31 2024 at 15:38):

I thought in Benno's suggestion, the context is a ZST, since we don't associate the irqflags (saved by local_irq_save()) with locks.

Boqun Feng (Jul 31 2024 at 15:40):

so the context is NOT a previous irq status.

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:40):

unlock does not need the context, but it might also need to return it to whatever code called and held the lock in the first place. That seems unrealistic considering the amount of places where you can just let a guard drop out of scope and never retrieve anything from it, but it came to mind that if the lock holds a context by value, it will need to decide what to do with it upon unlock.

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:41):

Again, apologizes if this is disjointed I discovered this patchset implementing an overlap of abstraction I've already written five hours ago and I'm getting somewhat familiar with it but the abstract usecase it's intended for is still somewhat fuzzy for me.

Boqun Feng (Jul 31 2024 at 15:43):

Maybe show an example of your version of the abstraction? That could help.

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:43):

With the example of the IrqDisabled token, which is one form a context could take, what happens upon unlock of a SpinLockIrq? Should the calling code retrieve the token because IRQs are still disabled, or should the SpinLockIrq's guard also drop the token, which as far as I understand re-enables IRQs. The latter seems easier to me, but also wrong.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 15:43):

Amélie Gonzalez said:

unlock does not need the context, but it might also need to return it to whatever code called and held the lock in the first place. That seems unrealistic considering the amount of places where you can just let a guard drop out of scope and never retrieve anything from it, but it came to mind that if the lock holds a context by value, it will need to decide what to do with it upon unlock.

I think it might be quiet difficult to add context to unlock, since as you said it is called by the dropping of the guard and the user might not be able to provide a context at that time

Amélie Gonzalez said:

Again, apologizes if this is disjointed I discovered this patchset implementing an overlap of abstraction I've already written five hours ago and I'm getting somewhat familiar with it but the abstract usecase it's intended for is still somewhat fuzzy for me.

no worries, do you have any concrete code that you can show that might help.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 15:44):

Amélie Gonzalez said:

With the example of the IrqDisabled token, which is one form a context could take, what happens upon unlock of a SpinLockIrq? Should the calling code retrieve the token because IRQs are still disabled, or should the SpinLockIrq's guard also drop the token, which as far as I understand re-enables IRQs. The latter seems easier to me, but also wrong.

dropping the IrqDisabled does not re-enable IRQ, it can just be dropped

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:45):

Boqun Feng said:

Maybe show an example of your version of the abstraction? That could help.

All my IRQ code concerns registration for now, sadly, no disabling :( i was hoping to use Lyude's work for that + spinlock_irqsave but the criticism of it make sense and while my code will never end up in RFL, i still want to stick to what other people are doing as much as possible.

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:46):

Benno Lossin said:

dropping the IrqDisabled does not re-enable IRQ, it can just be dropped

That seems unintuitive but I guess i missed something in the code and i don't get what the token is for, i will go read the code again and try and see if it makes more sense to me.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 15:46):

the way IRQs are dis/reen-abled is the following:

pub fn with_irqs_disabled<T, F>(cb: F) -> T
where
    F: FnOnce(IrqDisabled<'_>) -> T,
{
    // SAFETY: FFI call with no special requirements
    let flags = unsafe { bindings::local_irq_save() };

    let ret = cb(IrqDisabled(PhantomData));

    // SAFETY: `flags` comes from our previous call to local_irq_save
    unsafe { bindings::local_irq_restore(flags) };

    ret
}

And you would lock an SpinLockIrq within the cb. So

with_irq_disabled(|irq| {
    let guard = lock.lock_with(irq);
    // use guard here
});

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:47):

Oh, so the token's existence is a sign that you are within an IRQ-disabled closure, not an IRQ-disable guard

Boqun Feng (Jul 31 2024 at 15:47):

Yep

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:47):

Smarter than anything I could come up with

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:48):

I'll take that. But then that also imposes the same limitation I mentioned above for all contexts passed with unlock: dropping the context should have no side effect.

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:49):

Perhaps that should be documented in the associated type Backend::Context, i don't know if Lyude intends on doing so, but i would recommend it.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 15:51):

hmm I am not too sure about that, what would you count as a side-effect? eg I can print something and that shouldn't hurt, but for functional language people that would be a side-effect

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:54):

Side-effect in the sense of altering a state in the kernel in a way that should not be done unintentionally, is, i guess, the best way i would describe it? Sorry if it's too vague.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 15:55):

hmm I don't think I understand

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:55):

Disabling/Enabling IRQs would be a somewhat exaggerated example of a side effect you do not want to have upon dropping a lock, for example.

Boqun Feng (Jul 31 2024 at 15:56):

I think the difference is whether the context thingy can handle nested entering and exiting.

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:57):

Yes, that would be related to it I think.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 15:57):

I don't actually think that there is any problem, eg you are allowed to use Guard as the context and require that some other lock is locked before you can lock the other lock.

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:58):

If your context thingamabob can be used by multiple locks, it's either Clone or Copy, in which case it should not be used to structurally alter what's happening in scope (again, with the example of a guard)

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 15:58):

you cannot implement Drop when you implement Copy.

Boqun Feng (Jul 31 2024 at 15:58):

Like, if local_irq_save() is designed in a different way, for example, using a percpu/per-task counter to tracking whether irq is disable or not, the context handling of the old design of SpinLockIrqSave would be not a problem.

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 15:58):

I forgot that, that's true (re: Benno's comment).

Boqun Feng (Jul 31 2024 at 15:59):

but it's really a vague requirement.

Boqun Feng (Jul 31 2024 at 15:59):

I'd suggest we leave it as it is right now, and revisit later.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 16:00):

I don't actually think that a Context of the backend has any additional requirements, you could just as well safely implement your own drop guard and that should also work with any type.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 16:01):

you need to ensure that the tokens you create can handle nesting

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 16:01):

and then everything should be fine

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 16:01):

Yeah, that's true. Disregard my comments, thanks for giving me some more insight into the patchset.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 16:03):

no worries, feel free to ask again if you have any questions

Boqun Feng (Jul 31 2024 at 16:09):

Benno Lossin said:

you need to ensure that the tokens you create can handle nesting

It won't hurt having something mentioning this in the mod doc of lock.rs tho ;-)

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 16:10):

but this shouldn't be lock specific, if the token doesn't support nesting, then this is also problematic:

let a = disable_foo();
let b = disable_foo();
drop(b);
// foo is enabled, but `a` still exists!

Boqun Feng (Jul 31 2024 at 16:13):

It's going to be like a impl Backend checklist , which can save people some time for a better abstraction.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 16:14):

I am arguing the following: if your type is unsound when used as Backend::Context, then it is also unsound via the code above. Thus this is not a requirement we need to put on Backend, since every type should satisfy it anyways.

Boqun Feng (Jul 31 2024 at 16:15):

It won't hurt to have something that avoid unsound impl, right? I'm not talking about a safety requirement, rather than a "normal" documentation.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 16:16):

if we put this documentation somewhere, then it should be somewhere for general tips/info rather than lock-specific

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 16:17):

but if your type makes this unsound:
Benno Lossin said:

let a = disable_foo();
let b = disable_foo();
drop(b);
// foo is enabled, but `a` still exists!

then something went incredibly wrong

Boqun Feng (Jul 31 2024 at 16:20):

Benno Lossin said:

if we put this documentation somewhere, then it should be somewhere for general tips/info rather than lock-specific

Not sure I could agree on this, are you saying we should avoid documenting something unless the whole picture has been figured out? This seems to me like: you should start learn relativity theory instead of Newton's laws of motion for physics ;-)

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 16:24):

Boqun Feng said:

Benno Lossin said:

if we put this documentation somewhere, then it should be somewhere for general tips/info rather than lock-specific

Not sure I could agree on this, are you saying we should avoid documenting something unless the whole picture has been figured out? This seems to me like: you should start learn relativity theory instead of Newton's laws of motion for physics ;-)

I don't agree with that analogy, for me this would be more accurate: the docs are a sign that says "Do not cut your own arm with a chainsaw". You shouldn't even hold a chainsaw without the appropriate safety gear (ie cut-resistant clothing).
So yes we can document it, but IMO the state of the code is already so bad that you should never actually be in the scenario that the docs are useful

Boqun Feng (Jul 31 2024 at 16:30):

No, the doc would be "if you want to use chainsaw to cut the tree, here are the things you need to be careful about". People won't use a chainsaw for no reason.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 16:31):

if you want to submit a patch, I am not going to stop you :)

Boqun Feng (Jul 31 2024 at 16:32):

At very minimal, we have a few discussions on spin_lock_irqsave(), it'll be good to track it somewhere.

Benno Lossin (Jul 31 2024 at 16:33):

what we could put on with_irq_disabled would be the code example from above and say that that's why we can't use a token that just disables on creation and re-enables on drop.

Benno Lossin said:

if you want to submit a patch, I am not going to stop you :)

I was suggesting @Amélie Gonzalez to do it ;-) But I did say "won't hurt", so no pressure.

if you want to submit a patch, I am not going to stop you :)

Well I don't think there's any way my code or comments are remotely at kernel-quality, and Lyude will most likely have a better version available soon, so I find it more reasonable to pass on this one; I just wanted to give them a pointer on something like the code snippet about with a double guards and one that drops, which could be a problem for types that you shouldn't use as Backend context.

Also FWIW: there's no such thing as kernel quality :P. Everyone starts from somewhere, and the only way to sort of know what kernel maintainers are looking for is to learn first hand ♥.

Amélie Gonzalez (Jul 31 2024 at 21:28):

Inspirational, but I've read enough LKML over the years to not believe in fairy tales ^^

Everyone starts from somewhere, and the only way to sort of know what kernel maintainers are looking for is to learn first hand

Amélie Gonzalez (Aug 01 2024 at 09:27):

Oh and while I'm here (sorry to keep bothering you), on a slightly more meta point, did something change about the policy of reviewing abstractions without users while I was away from the project? I remember it being a point of confusion wrt net last year. Is this different because it's more of a general architecture abstraction for all parts of the kernel?

Alice Ryhl (Aug 01 2024 at 09:31):

In general, patches containing abstractions without a user should explain who the user will be. That is, it's okay to introduce them before the user, but you do still need to have an upcoming user.

Lyude (Aug 15 2024 at 21:41):

Small correction on my last email regarding the newly realized soundness issue: I don't think &mut would be the correct thing here, we probably just would want to pass IrqDisabled by value there as well and return another IrqDisabled

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 18:42):

@Boqun Feng and @Benno Lossin - btw, do we want to also remove the Copy trait from IrqDisabled and start using references?

Benno Lossin (Aug 16 2024 at 18:54):

Either we use &'a IrqDisabled (and remove the lifetime parameter [take care to still make it impossible to construct the struct via a private field]) or keep IrqDisabled<'a> with Copy. I don't really care about that. I only want consistency to avoid having to reborrow.

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 18:55):

Dropping the lifetime parameter seems fine to me

Boqun Feng (Aug 16 2024 at 18:55):

I'd suggest we use the references to start with, seems working and less complicated.

Benno Lossin (Aug 16 2024 at 18:56):

I guess one argument for using IrqDisabled<'a> would be that it always is a ZST (I don't know if references to ZSTs get special optimization treatment)

Benno Lossin (Aug 16 2024 at 18:56):

and when we use &'a IrqDisabled, then the question might arise whether an IrqDisabled by-value is useful at some time (it should not)

Benno Lossin (Aug 16 2024 at 18:56):

so I would slightly lean towards keeping IrqDisabled<'a> :)

Benno Lossin (Aug 16 2024 at 18:57):

but I don't mind switching to references.

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 18:57):

I think by-value would be useful sometimes. We can't fully prevent the soundness issue I mentioned before, but we can still make it less likely within safe code if rust functions that change interrupt state require you pass the IrqDisabled token by value

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 18:59):

That way you essentially end the lifetime of any kind of guards or such that rely on IRQs being disabled

Benno Lossin (Aug 16 2024 at 19:00):

but you can still nest calls and have the issue?

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 19:00):

Sure - but I'd think it's better then nothing no?

Benno Lossin (Aug 16 2024 at 19:00):

I think it makes it more dangerous, since API designers might not realize that you can create an arbitrary number of IrqDisabled tokens and users might then create multiple to work around the compiler error

Boqun Feng (Aug 16 2024 at 19:01):

Creating IrqDisabled should be unsafe anyway.

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 19:01):

Yeah - I think creating multiple to get around the error is a lot less likely then the nested calls potentially causing problems

Boqun Feng (Aug 16 2024 at 19:02):

So I think API designers will be well aware

Boqun Feng (Aug 16 2024 at 19:06):

We could also be very explicit about this, just add something in the doc of IrqDisabled: any function or struct that takes a IrqDisabled instead of &IrqDisabled should be defined as unsafe (or constructor needs to be unsafe) in most cases.

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 19:08):

Boqun Feng said:

We could also be very explicit about this, just add something in the doc of IrqDisabled: any function or struct that takes a IrqDisabled instead of &IrqDisabled should be defined as unsafe (or constructor needs to be unsafe) in most cases.

I still kinda like the idea I had before with functions that disable interrupt state on their own could take Option<IrqDisabled> and return Option<IrqDisabled> - since that at least provides some level of protection against nesting issues. Of course, we'd still need functions like that to be marked unsafe for the time being like Boqun mentioned

Boqun Feng (Aug 16 2024 at 19:12):

btw, be sure to Copy tglx next time, I'm happy to hear what he thinks about this

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 19:13):

sure thing

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 19:13):

tglx = thomas gleixner? (hopefully I spelled that right)

Boqun Feng (Aug 16 2024 at 19:13):

Yes

Boqun Feng (Aug 16 2024 at 19:19):

Oh, before I forget, just checking with you @Lyude , your patch covers one half of irq safe spinlock (disabling irqs in the process context), for the other half (locking in irq context), I assume you expect users to call IrqDisabled::new() to get a IrqDisabled and pass it to the lock_with(), right?

Benno Lossin (Aug 16 2024 at 19:21):

@Boqun Feng @Lyude do you guys mind sharing your current API idea here? I am a bit lost at what your exact proposal is at the moment.

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 19:22):

Boqun Feng said:

Oh, before I forget, just checking with you Lyude , your patch covers one half of irq safe spinlock (disabling irqs in the process context), for the other half (locking in irq context), I assume you expect users to call IrqDisabled::new() to get a IrqDisabled and pass it to the lock_with(), right?

Only in situations where the programmers knows that interrupts are already disabled (we have a few of these with KMS vblank interrupt handlers already), but yes

Boqun Feng (Aug 16 2024 at 19:23):

Lyude said:

Boqun Feng said:

Oh, before I forget, just checking with you Lyude , your patch covers one half of irq safe spinlock (disabling irqs in the process context), for the other half (locking in irq context), I assume you expect users to call IrqDisabled::new() to get a IrqDisabled and pass it to the lock_with(), right?

Only in situations where the programmers knows that interrupts are already disabled (we have a few of these with KMS vblank interrupt handlers already), but yes

We don't support nested interrupt in Linux kernel*, so in an irq handler, interrupt is always disabled.

[*]: Unless for some legacy devices.

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 19:24):

Boqun Feng said:

Lyude said:

Boqun Feng said:

Oh, before I forget, just checking with you Lyude , your patch covers one half of irq safe spinlock (disabling irqs in the process context), for the other half (locking in irq context), I assume you expect users to call IrqDisabled::new() to get a IrqDisabled and pass it to the lock_with(), right?

Only in situations where the programmers knows that interrupts are already disabled (we have a few of these with KMS vblank interrupt handlers already), but yes

We don't support nested interrupt in Linux kernel*, so in an irq handler, interrupt is always disabled.

[*]: Unless for some legacy devices.

Yes - which is why in such situations you'd want to use IrqDisabled::new() then

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 19:33):

Benno Lossin said:

Boqun Feng Lyude do you guys mind sharing your current API idea here? I am a bit lost at what your exact proposal is at the moment.

I will type up an explainer in a bit, I've gotta head out of the house though so it'll be a little later tonight.

Boqun Feng (Aug 16 2024 at 19:33):

Benno Lossin said:

Boqun Feng Lyude do you guys mind sharing your current API idea here? I am a bit lost at what your exact proposal is at the moment.

Something like below, maybe?

// !Send and !Sync
struct IrqDisabled(PhantomData<*mut ()>);


impl IrqDisabled {
    pub fn unsafe new() -> Self {
        debug_assert!(unsafe { !bindings::irq_disabled() });
        IrqDisabled(PhantomData)
    }
}

impl<...> Lock<...> {
    pub fn lock_with(&self, &IrqDisabled) -> Guard<..> { ... }
}

/// # Safety
/// Callers need to ensure `cb` returns with the same irq disabled state.
pub unsafe fn with_irqs_disabled(cb: for<'a> impl FnOnce(&'a IrqDisabled) -> T) -> T {
    let irq_save = unsafe { bindings::local_irq_save() };

    // SAFETY: IRQ is disabled by above.
    let id = unsafe { IrqDisabled::new() };
    let t = cb(&id);
    drop(id);

    // SAFETY: Pairs with the above `local_irq_save()`, and per function safety requirement, the irq disable state is the same as it is after the above `local_irq_save()`.
    unsafe { bindings::local_irq_restore(irq_save); }

    t
}

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 19:35):

Boqun Feng said:

Benno Lossin said:

Boqun Feng Lyude do you guys mind sharing your current API idea here? I am a bit lost at what your exact proposal is at the moment.

Something like below, maybe?

// !Send and !Sync
struct IrqDisabled(PhantomData<*mut ()>);


impl IrqDisabled {
    pub fn unsafe new() -> Self {
        debug_assert!(unsafe { !bindings::irq_disabled() });
        IrqDisabled(PhantomData)
    }
}

impl<...> Lock<...> {
    pub fn lock_with(&self, &IrqDisabled) -> Guard<..> { ... }
}

/// # Safety
/// Callers need to ensure `cb` returns with the same irq disabled state.
pub unsafe fn with_irqs_disabled(cb: for<'a> impl FnOnce(&'a IrqDisabled) -> T) -> T {
    let irq_save = unsafe { bindings::local_irq_save() };

    // SAFETY: IRQ is disabled by above.
    let id = unsafe { IrqDisabled::new() };
    let t = cb(&id);
    drop(id);

    // SAFETY: Pairs with the above `local_irq_save()`, and per function safety requirement, the irq disable state is the same as it is after the above `local_irq_save()`.
    unsafe { bindings::local_irq_restore(irq_save); }

    t
}

Yep - essentially. We could even go a step further in some functions where we know that IRQs should never be re-enabled (like the interrupt handlers that you mentioned) and pass the token as a reference so that functions that take an IrqDisabled by value (and as such, re-enable interrupts temporarily) can't be called

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 19:36):

It's still not perfect with the nesting issue we previously mentioned, but IMO some safety is better then none

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 19:36):

Anyway - gotta head out, bbl

Benno Lossin (Aug 16 2024 at 19:47):

hmm what is the safety contract of local_irq_save and local_irq_restore?

Boqun Feng (Aug 16 2024 at 19:52):

For local_irq_save(), there are no safety requirement: sleeping with irq disabled is something klint should catch (sorry, klint). For local_irq_restore(), there must be a previous local_irq_save(), and the interrupt state must be the same as after local_irq_save() is called.

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 20:51):

farnsworth voice good news everyone! I think I might have actually figured out a solution to the nesting problem we mentioned before that doesn't seem too unreasonable. Going to write something up real quick to explain

Trevor Gross (Aug 16 2024 at 21:20):

I do think that IrqDisabled<'a> is better than &'a IrqDisabled because it makes it makes the relationship to lifetime more obvious. Along the lines of MutexGuard<'a, ..> or Entry<'a, ..> (though admittedly these all have their own self APIs, IrqDisabled won't). I also like the explicit Copy, but that's less relevant.

Trevor Gross (Aug 16 2024 at 21:21):

Perhaps I am just biased coming from using critical_section::CriticalSection<'a> everywhere in embedded :)

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 21:21):

I think it's going to likely end up being a ref tbh, I'm still writing it up but I realized having both by-ref and by-value would let us expose some of the IRQ disable stuff as safe functions

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 21:21):

but only some of it

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 22:06):

Benno Lossin said:

Boqun Feng Lyude do you guys mind sharing your current API idea here? I am a bit lost at what your exact proposal is at the moment.

So, before I explain I'm going to list some of the important facts to keep in mind while reading this:

  • Interrupt disables can be and commonly are nested
  • If you see "IRQ ??", this means "IRQs may or may not be on in this context".

  • There are two different types of contexts where interrupts can be disabled:
    Context A: In response to a hardware interrupt - in this context, interrupts can -never- be turned back on
    Context B: After being manually disabled from a state where they were previously on - interrupts can be re-enabled

  • Any kind of lock, say a spinlock, which may be acquired in a no-IRQ context must -always- be acquired in a no-irq context

  • Any kind of lock acquired in a no-IRQ context must be dropped before interrupts are enabled. In other words, the lifetime of its guard is tied to the lifetime of IrqDisabled. We'll call this "the spinlock rule".
  • We will make one of the safety requirements of IrqDisabled::new() be "the caller must never create multiple of these tokens within the same scope"

Now recall that we've also defined a SpinLockIrq, in this write-up locking SpinLockIrq will look like this:

  some_spinlockirq.lock_with(&'a irq) -> Guard<'a, …>

Furthermore, we can classify anything in the kernel that relies or changes interrupt state into three groups:

  • IRQs off -> do work -> IRQs off
    This is the simplest one: we know we started with interrupts disabled, so we can't assume we won't break the spinlock rule if we turn them back on since we didn't disable them in the first place. So, "do work" can only contain nested IRQ disables

  • IRQs ?? -> IRQs off -> do work -> IRQs ??
    This is a bit more complicated, but follows the same rule as the previous group. Because interrupts -could- be disabled, we lack enough context to know that turning interrupts back on would not break the spinlock rule. Thus - "do work" can only contain nested IRQ disables.

  • IRQs on -> IRQs off -> do work (can contain IRQs on -> IRQs off) -> IRQs on
    Since we started with IRQs enabled and disabled them ourselves, this means have enough context to know that so long as we drop any spinlocks we acquired after "IRQs off" we can safely re-enable interrupts without breaking the spinlock rule.

I'll start by handling the third group, since this is important to understand for handling the first and second group. We would handle this group using with_irqs_disabled():

// SAFETY: `irq` must be Some() if IRQs are enabled when this function is called, or None otherwise
pub unsafe fn with_irqs_disabled<T>(
  irq: Option<IrqDisabled>,
  cb: impl FnOnce(IrqDisabled) -> T,
) -> (Option<IrqDisabled>, T) {
  …
}

Here, IrqDisabled is passed to the callback by value. But with a caveat - the user is asked to provide an Option<IrqDisabled> which represents the -current- IRQ state as part of the safety contract for with_irqs_disabled. We can also use a debug assertion to verify this at runtime to help catch mistakes.

The advantage of requiring the user pass an IrqDisabled here is simple: the user is required to only ever have one of these tokens in scope if interrupts are disabled, via the safety contract of IrqDisabled::new. And since the user needs to pass their token (if they have one) to this function by value, no spinlock guards which we acquired using the token will be alive once interrupts are enabled. Thus, we don't violate the spinlock rule even with nested calls.

Furthermore, we can require that any function which goes from IRQs off -> IRQs on must take an IrqDisabled. As an example, let's create an imaginary binding for wait_event_lock_interruptible_irq():

pub fn wait_event_lock_interruptible_irq(irq: IrqDisabled, lock: &SpinLockIrq<…>, cb: impl FnOnce()) -> IrqDisabled {
  …
}

Since we've taken an IrqDisabled by value, we can be sure that we've ended the lifetime shared by any Guards which we acquired while interrupts were disabled. We also know that either we were the ones who disabled interrupts originally, or that any earlier context which disabled interrupts has already dropped any Guards which relied on interrupts being disabled. Thus, we can confirm we haven't violated the spinlock rule.

Since this would kind of be a pain ergonomically, we can forgo this trouble entirely for the first and second group I previously mentioned - and even provide a safe function in this case:

pub fn with_irqs_always_disabled<T>(cb: impl FnOnce(&IrqDisabled) -> T) -> T {
  …
}

Since this function only passes IrqDisabled by reference, we can assume the following:

  • Whether IRQs were enabled or disabled when this function was called is irrelevant - as it is impossible with safe code to re-enable them within cb() since we only have a reference to IrqDisabled.
  • Since we can nest IRQ disables, with_irqs_always_disabled() can also be safely nested since IRQ enables are impossible within cb()
  • Thus, the spinlock rule cannot be violated with this function using safe code :)

Lyude (Aug 16 2024 at 22:08):

yay ♥

Benno Lossin (Aug 17 2024 at 12:04):

I haven't yet read through your writeup, but I noticed the following: why do we need with_irq_disabled to be unsafe? There is no safe function to re-enable IRQs and if we never add one, then the with_irq_disabled can be safe.

Benno Lossin (Aug 17 2024 at 12:55):

First a couple inline comments:

Lyude said:

// SAFETY: `irq` must be Some() if IRQs are enabled when this function is called, or None otherwise
pub unsafe fn with_irqs_disabled<T>(
  irq: Option<IrqDisabled>,
  cb: impl FnOnce(IrqDisabled) -> T,
) -> (Option<IrqDisabled>, T) {
  …
}

I think the safety comment should read "irq must be None if IRQs are enabled or Some(_) otherwise.".

Furthermore, we can require that any function which goes from IRQs off -> IRQs on must take an IrqDisabled. As an example, let's create an imaginary binding for wait_event_lock_interruptible_irq():

(For anyone searching for that function, its name is wait_event_interruptible_locked_irq)

pub fn wait_event_lock_interruptible_irq(irq: IrqDisabled, lock: &SpinLockIrq<…>, cb: impl FnOnce()) -> IrqDisabled {
  …
}

Shouldn't this take a Guard<'_, T, SpinLockIrqBackend> instead of the spinlock? Also where is the condition and why is there a callback?

So I think there are some problems with what you propose:

// SAFETY: we statically know that IRQs are disabled
let (_, irq) = unsafe { with_irqs_disabled(None, |irq| irq) };
// now we have a token, but IRQs are enabled!

So I think we need to change it to be

pub unsafe fn with_irqs_disabled<T>(
    irq: Option<IrqDisabled>,
    cb: impl FnOnce(IrqDisabled) -> (IrqDisabled, T),
) -> (Option<IrqDisabled>, T);

Another problem is the following: If I design the following function (I think this is what you tried to do with wait_event_lock_interruptible_irq`):

pub fn do_unlocked_irq<'a, T, R>(
    irq: IrqDisabled,
    guard: Guard<'a, T, SpinLockIrqBackend>,
    cb: impl FnOnce() -> R,
) -> (IrqDisabled, Guard<'a, T, SpinLockIrqBackend>, R);

Then how would the user be able to pass an IrqDisabled together with the Guard? The guard's lifetime should be tied to the tokens', so it wouldn't compile.

But actually with the way it's currently designed, you can call it (but it is obviously wrong):

let lock: &SpinLock<T> = ...;
let cb = |irq1| {
    with_irqs_always_disabled(|irq2| {
        let guard = lock.lock_with(irq2);
        let _ = do_unlocked(irq1, guard, || {
            let guard = lock.lock_with(irq2); // whoops, we can lock despite IRQs being enabled! (also `&IrqDisabled` exists!)
        });
    });
};
// SAFETY: we statically know IRQs are enabled
unsafe { with_irqs_disabled(None, cb) };

Benno Lossin (Aug 17 2024 at 12:57):

what I think we can do is to keep the design from v3, since there are no safe functions to re-enable IRQs. when we eventually introduce them, then we can make those functions unsafe and the caller needs to ensure that IRQs are only re-enabled when its expected (though we should spend some time now to figure out if this is indeed a good idea!)

Benno Lossin (Aug 17 2024 at 12:59):

oh and of course thanks for creating that extensive writeup!

Lyude (Aug 17 2024 at 18:32):

no problem! JFYI I realized I forgot to mention - at the moment I only really have code that would use with_irqs_disabled_always - I kind of just included the other version of the function to make it easier to understand.

Benno Lossin (Aug 17 2024 at 18:34):

sure, your original patch also only includes that. But I think we should at least consider how we would expand this API in the future. because if we select something now that is difficult to extend we will run into trouble later.

Benno Lossin (Aug 17 2024 at 18:34):

so it's good that we had/(continue to have) this discussion :)

Dirk Behme (Aug 19 2024 at 05:29):

The above verbose write up triggered my attention and made me having a look to the [PATCH v3 0/3] rust: Add irq abstraction, SpinLockIrq patch series as well. With that I'd like to ask/mention/understand three topics :grinning:

First, many thanks for this verbose write up! I think we need more like this :wink: To not loose this in the archives of Zulip I wonder if it could be made somehow more persistent? Would it be an option to add something like this for example as a 4th patch to the patch series in e.g. Documentation/rust/spinlock_irq_design.rst?

Second, regarding wording of enabled/disabled interrupts. While reading the write up I stopped at "no-IRQ context". I think a "context where IRQs are disabled" is meant here? That made me having an attention to the wording :wink: . It seems in the write up and patches we at least have

  • "IRQs off/on"
  • "IRQs disabled/enabled"
  • "no-IRQ context"
  • "IRQ-free context"
  • "running with/without interrupts"

I think all this describes the same. I wonder if we should be more consistent and use less variants for the same? Personally, I'm mainly thinking in disabled/enabled or on/off interrupts. But of course this is quite personal taste :grinning:

And third, I'd like to have a look to the patch series from user point of view. Not from design/API/safety/soundness point of view like the write up is mainly about. But how this is supposed to be used:

With the kernel C's spin_lock_irqsave()/spin_unlock_irqrestore() we are defining sections where IRQs are disabled and which are lock protected for synchronization. In these interrupt disabled sections you can do "whatever you want" as long as you do nothing what is forbidden (like sleeping, programming deadlocks etc). What not necessarily must be shared data manipulation, only.

Now, looking at the example from [PATCH v3 3/3] rust: sync: Add SpinLockIrq :

use kernel::{
    sync::{new_spinlock_irq, SpinLockIrq},
    irq::{with_irqs_disabled, IrqDisabled}
};

struct Inner {
    a: u32,
    b: u32,
}

#[pin_data]
struct Example {
    c: u32,
    #[pin]
    d: SpinLockIrq<Inner>,
}

impl Example {
    fn new() -> impl PinInit<Self> {
        pin_init!(Self {
            c: 10,
            d <- new_spinlock_irq!(Inner { a: 20, b: 30 }),
        })
    }
}

// Accessing an `Example` from a function that can only be called in no-irq contexts
fn noirq_work(e: &Example, irq: IrqDisabled<'_>) {
    assert_eq!(e.c, 10);
    assert_eq!(e.d.lock_with(irq).a, 20);
}

// Allocate a boxed `Example`
let e = Box::pin_init(Example::new(), GFP_KERNEL)?;

// Accessing an `Example` from a context where IRQs may not be disabled already.
let b = with_irqs_disabled(|irq| {
    noirq_work(&e, irq);
    e.d.lock_with(irq).b
});
assert_eq!(b, 30);
# Ok::<(), Error>(())
  • The kernel C's interrupt spin lock protected & interrupt disabled section becomes a function, in the example noirq_work(), now?
  • The locking/interrupt disable/enable needs to be bound to some kind of data? In the example struct Inner?

I'm wondering how a synchronization example would look like where the data synchronization is not the main reason for the lock? For example lets assume a hardware driver which might need to synchronize some hardware access like

Thread #A                                      Thread #B

...                                            ...
spin_lock_irqsave(&lock, flags);               spin_lock_irqsave(&lock, flags);
write_to_register(reg_A, 0xdead);              write_to_register(reg_A, 0xbeef);
wirte_to_register(reg_B, 0xbeef);              wirte_to_register(reg_B, 0xdead);
spin_unlock_irqrestore(&lock, flags);          spin_unlock_irqrestore(&lock, flags);
...                                            ...

How would that look like using the proposal from here?

Please don't get me wrong. I don't say that something is wrong or should be changed. I just want to understand the differences to what we are used to from C and how this is supposed to be used :grinning:

Boqun Feng (Aug 19 2024 at 05:34):

You probably need something like SpinLockIrq<Regs>, where Regs is:

struct Regs {
    a: IoMem,
    b: IoMem,
}

(IoMem is made up by me, it represents mmio registers)

Boqun Feng (Aug 19 2024 at 05:36):

And then you could do:

with_irqs_disabled(|irq| {
    let regs: &SpinLockIrq<Regs> = ...;
    g = regs.lock_with(irq);
    g.a.write(0xdead); // write() is a &mut function
    g.a.write(0xbeef);
})

Boqun Feng (Aug 19 2024 at 05:41):

If for some reaons, you cannot introduce Regs, you could either use locked_by or just use unsafe:

Example for unsafe:

with_irqs_disabled(|irq| {
    let regs_lock: &SpinLockIrq<()> = .. ; // some lock provides the exclusive access to `reg_A` and `reg_B`.
    let g = regs_lock.lock_with(irq);
    // SAFETY: `regs_lock` provide the exclusive access to `reg_A`.
    unsafe { write_to_register(reg_A, 0xdead); } // <- this function will be defined as unsafe since exclusive access to `reg_A` is required.
});

Boqun Feng (Aug 19 2024 at 05:48):

So regardless of hardware registers or shared memory, if you want to have exclusvie accesses, you will need the lock.

Boqun Feng (Aug 19 2024 at 05:50):

So your example is the same as the data synchronization case.

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 19:15):

So: maybe there's something I'm misunderstanding here in regards to how lifetime parameters on types work. I know we mentioned that even if something like IrqDisabled<'a> is Copyable, you still can't copy it somewhere and keep it around beyond it's lifetime. But how then would we be able to end the lifetime 'a for a function that re-enables IRQs if a copy of the token still exists in the function? Like:

with_irqs_disabled(|irq: IrqDisabled<'a>| {
  let second_irq = irq;
  let spinlock = some_irqspinlock.lock_with(irq);

  reenable_irqs(irq);
  // second_irq is still present - so wouldn't 'a still be valid here?
}

Benno Lossin (Aug 21 2024 at 19:18):

that's the neat part, you can't :)

but that's not a "real" problem, since this code has the same problem (and it still works with the token not being Copy):

with_irqs_disabled(|irq| {
    with_irqs_disabled(|irq2| {
        let guard = some_irqspinlock.lock_with(irq);
        reenable_irqs(irq);
        // still have irq2!
    })
})

So you can't really solve this with the type system. At least that is what my intuition is telling me, I don't have a proof yet :)

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 19:21):

That's why I mentioned we have some functions take it by reference and some by value. Remember, the with_irqs_disabled() actually had two arguments: irq: Option<IrqDisabled> and cb. With the safety contract being "you need to ensure that you pass Some(IrqDisabled) if you're calling the function from a context with interrupts disabled - since that allows us to actually end the lifetime and gives us runtime verification the situation you jsut mentioned isn't happening - at least for debug builds, since we could do something like debug_assert!(irq.is_some() == unsafe { bindings::irqs_disabled() })

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 19:22):

So -technically- the nesting problem could still happen, but it seems like it would be a lot less likely to happen since you're forcing the caller to figure out what context it's calling the function in - and giving things a way to blow up if they get it wrong in a debug build.

Benno Lossin (Aug 21 2024 at 19:22):

I don't like with_irqs_disabled being an unsafe function. We should make the easy and common situation safe.

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 19:23):

hence the with_irqs_disabled_always which -is- a safe function, and handles the more common case of only disabling IRQs multiple times

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 19:23):

And since that only takes it by reference, there's no way to go from with_irqs_disabled_always() to with_irqs_disabled() without invoking an unsafe block. But the more common case can simply stick to safe blocks and not worry about the nesting problem since it's all references.

Benno Lossin (Aug 21 2024 at 19:24):

But then you have the problem that I detailed above:

Benno Lossin said:

let lock: &SpinLock<T> = ...;
let cb = |irq1| {
    with_irqs_always_disabled(|irq2| {
        let guard = lock.lock_with(irq2);
        let _ = do_unlocked(irq1, guard, || {
            let guard = lock.lock_with(irq2); // whoops, we can lock despite IRQs being enabled! (also `&IrqDisabled` exists!)
        });
    });
};
// SAFETY: we statically know IRQs are enabled
unsafe { with_irqs_disabled(None, cb) };

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 19:25):

Oh I totally forgot about do_unlocked.
Though, maybe we could just make do_unlocked() only available where B::Context: Default? Having to re-enable interrupts is essentially already an unlock already, though, if you wanted to unlock and not re-enable interrupts I suppose that would complicate things

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 19:26):

what if do_unlocked had a do_unlocked_with?

Benno Lossin (Aug 21 2024 at 19:26):

you can replace do_unlocked with any function that re-enables irqs

Benno Lossin (Aug 21 2024 at 19:26):

and the problem still exists

Benno Lossin (Aug 21 2024 at 19:26):

let lock: &SpinLock<T> = ...;
let cb = |irq1| {
    with_irqs_always_disabled(|irq2| {
        let guard = lock.lock_with(irq2);
        re_enable_irqs(irq);
        let guard = lock.lock_with(irq2); // whoops, we can lock despite IRQs being enabled! (also `&IrqDisabled` exists!)
    });
};
// SAFETY: we statically know IRQs are enabled
unsafe { with_irqs_disabled(None, cb) };

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 19:33):

(JFYI: I'm ignoring the ZST reference optimization problem for a minute here, since I think that might make a bit more sense to solve once we figure out the requirement of safe/unsafe here)
Something that's confusing me a bit about your example. I assume re_enable_irqs(irq) is a typo, but - we would only ever want any function that passes an IrqDisabled by value to itself also be unsafe. Things like interrupt handlers for instance would only ever want to pass it as a reference.
So - if the only way to get IrqDisabled by value is from unsafe functions, presumably any area where interrupts are already disabled that didn't originate from an unsafe callback would not have access to a IrqDisabled by value. Thus if you tried calling with_irqs_disabled(irq, cb) in one of those contexts you wouldn't have an IrqDisabled by value - only by reference. Which means irq would have to be None - which doesn't match the actual interrupt state (Some(IrqDisabled)) - and thus would trigger the debug assertion.

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 19:34):

And also violate the safety contract, of course

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 19:35):

So like - at least within the context of just safe blocks, I'm not sure how you'd be able to call with_irqs_disabled() within a with_irqs_disabled_always() closure unless you use an unsafe block

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 19:40):

Now, you could do something like:

unsafe {
  with_irqs_disabled(None, |irq| {
    with_irqs_disabled_always(|irq2| {
      // mischevious safety violations....
    }
  }
}

But that still requires you start with an unsafe block - so we could disallow that as part of the safety contract for with_irqs_disabled()

Benno Lossin (Aug 21 2024 at 19:41):

Lyude said:

(JFYI: I'm ignoring the ZST reference optimization problem for a minute here, since I think that might make a bit more sense to solve once we figure out the requirement of safe/unsafe here)
Something that's confusing me a bit about your example. I assume re_enable_irqs(irq) is a typo, but - we would only ever want any function that passes an IrqDisabled by value to itself also be unsafe. Things like interrupt handlers for instance would only ever want to pass it as a reference.

No, it's not a typo, I thought the reason for making with_irqs_disabled unsafe is to be able to make functions that re-enable IRQs safe.

So - if the only way to get IrqDisabled by value is from unsafe functions, presumably any area where interrupts are already disabled that didn't originate from an unsafe callback would not have access to a IrqDisabled by value. Thus if you tried calling with_irqs_disabled(irq, cb) in one of those contexts you wouldn't have an IrqDisabled by value - only by reference. Which means irq would have to be None - which doesn't match the actual interrupt state (Some(IrqDisabled)) - and thus would trigger the debug assertion.

I don't understand this part, in the above example, I am not calling with_irqs_disabled with Some. Also how do you enforce that people make functions that take IrqDisabled unsafe? What should the safety requirements be?

Lyude said:

So like - at least within the context of just safe blocks, I'm not sure how you'd be able to call with_irqs_disabled() within a with_irqs_disabled_always() closure unless you use an unsafe block

I am calling them the other way around.

Benno Lossin (Aug 21 2024 at 19:42):

Lyude said:

Now, you could do something like:

unsafe {
  with_irqs_disabled(None, |irq| {
    with_irqs_disabled_always(|irq2| {
      // mischevious safety violations....
    }
  }
}

But that still requires you start with an unsafe block - so we could disallow that as part of the safety contract for with_irqs_disabled()

yeah, we could, but what does this give us? what is the advantage, I don't really see it

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 19:46):

oh! I think I see an issue here that might be leading to confusion, sec

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 20:02):

Benno Lossin said:

No, it's not a typo, I thought the reason for making with_irqs_disabled unsafe is to be able to make functions that re-enable IRQs safe.

Also jfyi - no, I don't think we would ever want any function that re-enables IRQs to be safe tbh. At least not until we have a klint thing.

Benno Lossin (Aug 21 2024 at 20:06):

yeah I agree, but then I don't think we need the two functions to disable IRQs, one safe one should be enough (and also only one token kind)

Benno Lossin (Aug 21 2024 at 20:06):

essentially, your v3 proposal on the list is then exactly what we would like to have

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 20:12):

So:

Benno Lossin said:

let lock: &SpinLock<T> = ...;
let cb = |irq1| {
    with_irqs_always_disabled(|irq2| {
        let guard = lock.lock_with(irq2);
        re_enable_irqs(irq);
        let guard = lock.lock_with(irq2); // whoops, we can lock despite IRQs being enabled! (also `&IrqDisabled` exists!)
    });
};
// SAFETY: we statically know IRQs are enabled
unsafe { with_irqs_disabled(None, cb) };

Keeping in mind we only have two ways of creating an owned IrqDisabled - with_irqs_disabled() and IrqDisabled::new() which are both unsafe functions. Now, we could make with_irqs_disabled() say in its safety contract that any function calling this must:

  • Be unsafe itself
  • accept a Option<IrqDisabled> and pass it directly to with_irqs_disabled(). Then, with_irqs_disabled() would return Option<IrqDisabled> which should be equivalent to the input irq argument (e.g. if we pass Some(IrqDisabled) in, it should give us Some(IrqDisabled) out - and vice versa for None)
  • We would also require that at the end of with_irqs_disabled() the state of interrupts must match what it was when the function was originally called. So if it does re-enable interrupts, it's required to disable them by the time it returns
  • And finally, require that any function calling with_irqs_disabled() needs to return the result of with_irqs_disabled() (e.g. the Option<IrqDisabled>).

For IrqDisabled::new() - we could simply make it in the safety contract that any function using this must not have disabled interrupts itself unless it follows the safety contract of with_irqs_disabled().

So - going back to your example, if re_enable_irqs() enables interrupts through with_irqs_disabled() - then this code would be in violation of the contract unless it takes a Option<IrqDisabled> by value and returns it, and by the safety contract that value must match the interrupt state at the start of the function.

The advantage of this complexity is that with all of this, because we require a function that re-enables interrupts to consume a Option<IrqDisabled> from its parent context - we would be ending the lifetime of any resources that were acquired using a reference to IrqDisabled. Which means that (hopefully I'm not misunderstanding how rust handles this) we would be forcing spinlocks held under interrupt to be dropped by the time interrupts are turned on. And trying to use nesting to avoid this requirement would just simply be in violation of that safety contract because of the need for irq: Option<IrqDisabled> to represent the starting interrupt state.

Then of course, the far more common case of the safe function with_irqs_disabled_always() which simply deals in references and doesn't care about the starting interrupt state would only have references to IrqDisabled. Thus, since we can only generate an IrqDisabled token by-value using unsafe code, that would make it impossible to generate the Option<IrqDisabled> for with_irqs_disabled() without violating the safety contract of with_irqs_disabled(). Since the only other way of creating the token requires that the function not control interrupts itself (via the requirements of IrqDisabled::new(), or follow the safety contract of with_irqs_disabled().

sorry for all the complexity here btw!

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 20:33):

I'm willing to write up a PoC if folks want to play around with this to make sure my logic here is sound btw. Maybe that would be a bit easier to understand? (and also tell me if I'm the one misunderstanding things here :)

Benno Lossin (Aug 21 2024 at 20:52):

Ah! I think I understand. sorry that took a while...

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 20:53):

haha no problem! it took me a while too :P

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 20:54):

honestly I think I spent a few hours coming up with that iirc

Benno Lossin (Aug 21 2024 at 20:55):

so the crux of my not-understanding was that I didn't think of

Which means that (hopefully I'm not misunderstanding how rust handles this) we would be forcing spinlocks held under interrupt to be dropped by the time interrupts are turned on.

Benno Lossin (Aug 21 2024 at 20:56):

so we still want guards to be dropped early enough, even in unsafe code

Benno Lossin (Aug 21 2024 at 20:56):

I will have to think a bit more if there is a more elegant design...

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 20:59):

sounds good to me :), yeah - it's definitely a bit much, my hope is that once klint is a thing we could make this more elegant

Lyude (Aug 21 2024 at 23:13):

Benno Lossin (Aug 23 2024 at 07:47):

Boqun Feng (Aug 23 2024 at 18:27):

Could you remember me what's the difference between with_irqs_disabled_always and with_irqs_disabled? always is safe and doesn't allow you to re-enable in the closure?

Gary Guo (Aug 23 2024 at 21:19):

Lyude said:

and yeah I don't currently have a user for it, guess I was mostly wondering if we at least maybe wanted to write this down somewhere so people know what to do when they do need it
Also - I guess regarding the ref vs . types thing that we were talking about the other day, I assume I should probably replace &IrqDisabled with some sort of IrqDisabledRef<'a> type?

I would rather to have the IrqDisabled<'a> as the ref (and Copy) type, and when and if people need the owned version, we can invent a new type called IrqDisabledOwned<'a> or something. I think the latter will be rarely used if used at all.

Dirk Behme (Aug 26 2024 at 07:19):

Boqun Feng said:

If for some reaons, you cannot introduce Regs, you could either use locked_by or just use unsafe:

Example for unsafe:

with_irqs_disabled(|irq| {
    let regs_lock: &SpinLockIrq<()> = .. ; // some lock provides the exclusive access to `reg_A` and `reg_B`.
    let g = regs_lock.lock_with(irq);
    // SAFETY: `regs_lock` provide the exclusive access to `reg_A`.
    unsafe { write_to_register(reg_A, 0xdead); } // <- this function will be defined as unsafe since exclusive access to `reg_A` is required.
});

Could you give a hint what you had in mind for .. in

 let regs_lock: &SpinLockIrq<()> = .. ;

?
I tried

with_irqs_disabled(|irq| {
     let regs_lock: &SpinLockIrq<()> = new_spinlock_irq!(());
     let mut _guard = regs_lock.lock_with(irq);
     unsafe { let _foo = 1; } // Do anything unsafe here
 });

but got

error[E0308]: mismatched types
   --> samples/rust/rust_spinlock.rs:76:47
    |
76  |             let regs_lock: &SpinLockIrq<()> = new_spinlock_irq!(());
    |                            ----------------   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ expected `&Lock<(), SpinLockIrqBackend>`, found opaque type
    |                            |
    |                            expected due to this
    |
   ::: rust/kernel/sync/lock.rs:117:74
    |
117 |     pub fn new(t: T, name: &'static CStr, key: &'static LockClassKey) -> impl PinInit<Self> {
    |                                                                          ------------------ the found opaque type
    |
    = note: expected reference `&Lock<(), SpinLockIrqBackend>`
             found opaque type `impl kernel::init::PinInit<Lock<(), SpinLockIrqBackend>>`
    = note: this error originates in the macro `new_spinlock_irq` (in Nightly builds, run with -Z macro-backtrace for more info)

Benno Lossin (Aug 26 2024 at 07:22):

new_spinlock_irq! is a macro that creates an initializer, so you still need to use it

Benno Lossin (Aug 26 2024 at 07:23):

for example by Box::pin_init or Arc::pin_init or if you need it on the stack with stack_pin_init!

Boqun Feng (Aug 26 2024 at 13:53):

Dirk Behme said:

Boqun Feng said:

If for some reaons, you cannot introduce Regs, you could either use locked_by or just use unsafe:

Example for unsafe:

with_irqs_disabled(|irq| {
    let regs_lock: &SpinLockIrq<()> = .. ; // some lock provides the exclusive access to `reg_A` and `reg_B`.
    let g = regs_lock.lock_with(irq);
    // SAFETY: `regs_lock` provide the exclusive access to `reg_A`.
    unsafe { write_to_register(reg_A, 0xdead); } // <- this function will be defined as unsafe since exclusive access to `reg_A` is required.
});

Could you give a hint what you had in mind for .. in

 let regs_lock: &SpinLockIrq<()> = .. ;

?

What I mean is this is the place where you reference the lock, and the lock must be created before this. One way to get a &SpinLock<()> is (assume the spinlock is defined in C already) from_raw() in Lyude's code.

Dirk Behme (Aug 27 2024 at 07:07):

Boqun Feng said:

What I mean is this is the place where you reference the lock, and the lock must be created before this. One way to get a &SpinLock<()> is (assume the spinlock is defined in C already) from_raw() in Lyude's code.

Ah, thanks! Lyude's from_raw() is not on the mailing list, on the mailing list there are only the first 3 patches. Therefore I wasn't aware of that. Many thanks, I'll have a look :grinning:

Dirk Behme (Aug 27 2024 at 11:46):

Regarding the &SpinLockIrq<()> are we talking about anything like this? At least it compiles. Just as example for getting a &SpinLockIrq<()> where the definition is in C (ignoring that there is no unlock ...)):

diff --git a/rust/helpers.c b/rust/helpers.c
index 0348ed99a2623..3abcc693bb16a 100644
--- a/rust/helpers.c
+++ b/rust/helpers.c
@@ -53,6 +53,17 @@ __noreturn void rust_helper_BUG(void)
 }
 EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(rust_helper_BUG);

+static DEFINE_SPINLOCK(test_lock);
+static unsigned long flags;
+
+spinlock_t *rust_helper_test_spinlock_irqsave(void)
+{
+       spin_lock_irqsave(&test_lock, flags);
+
+       return &test_lock;
+}
+EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(rust_helper_test_spinlock_irqsave);
+
 unsigned long rust_helper_copy_from_user(void *to, const void __user *from,
                                         unsigned long n)
 {
diff --git a/samples/rust/rust_minimal.rs b/samples/rust/rust_minimal.rs
index 3b918ff5eebbe..74888be542549 100644
--- a/samples/rust/rust_minimal.rs
+++ b/samples/rust/rust_minimal.rs
@@ -2,7 +2,9 @@

 //! Rust minimal sample.

+use kernel::bindings;
 use kernel::prelude::*;
+use kernel::sync::{SpinLockIrq, lock::Lock};

 module! {
     type: RustMinimal,
@@ -26,6 +28,8 @@ fn init(_name: &'static CStr, _module: &'static ThisModule) -> Result<Self> {
         numbers.push(108, GFP_KERNEL)?;
         numbers.push(200, GFP_KERNEL)?;

+        let _regs_lock: &SpinLockIrq<()> = unsafe{ Lock::from_raw(bindings::test_spinlock_irqsave()) };
+
         Ok(RustMinimal { numbers })
     }
 }

Benno Lossin (Aug 27 2024 at 11:49):

you wouldn't lock the lock when calling from_raw

Benno Lossin (Aug 27 2024 at 11:50):

but otherwise it should work like that.

but for now, I think you only need one (and just name it IrqDisabled)

Lyude (Aug 30 2024 at 21:42):

Lyude (Aug 30 2024 at 21:47):

aa, sorry for the questions but I am realizing I'm still a little confused. If we have IrqDisabled<'a> then we can't really pass that around like a reference to functions unless it's Copy, which brings up the issue we had before where I thought we agreed Copy wouldn't really work for this?

Lyude (Aug 30 2024 at 21:48):

I'm pretty sure that remains true even without the version of the API that would pass this by value

Boqun Feng (Aug 30 2024 at 21:51):

I think our conclusion is we need a Copy one for now (which acts as a reference), and if we were to have irq-reenable function, we ,may introduce a !Copy in the future.

Boqun Feng (Aug 30 2024 at 21:52):

but what's the issue that Copy doesn't work for?

Lyude (Aug 30 2024 at 21:53):

Boqun Feng said:

but what's the issue that Copy doesn't work for?

Precisely what you just said - and for more context it was around making sure that we can require re-enabling interrupts causes IrqDisabled to get revoked by passing it by-value. I forgot that Copy should work fine though so long as we don't have a chance that safe code can re-enable interrupts - but we will definitely have to refactor code later that uses this

Lyude (Aug 30 2024 at 21:53):

when we add such a capability I mean

Benno Lossin (Aug 30 2024 at 21:55):

Yeah that will have to be done. Feel free to create an issue for that (ie to track it)

Lyude (Aug 30 2024 at 21:55):

gotcha

Boqun Feng (Aug 30 2024 at 21:57):

but that shouldn't affect most of existing users, right? The impact I can foresee is having re-enabling API will change how we get the IrqDisabled<'a> (i.e. the irq disabled proof token), but most of the users should not get this token directly (they will get is from with_irqs_disabled() most of the time).

Lyude (Aug 30 2024 at 21:59):

thinking about it yeah you're right. Changing it from IrqDisabled<'a> to &'a IrqDisabled or IrqDisabledRef<'a> wouldn't make any difference so long as interrupts don't get re-enabled in safe code

Lyude (Sep 16 2024 at 21:02):

@Gary Guo I'm a bit confused here, you mentioned in reviewing my spinlock series:

> +    /// disabled at least until this [`IrqDisabled`] is dropped.
> +    pub unsafe fn new() -> Self {
> +        // SAFETY: FFI call with no special requirements
> +        debug_assert!(unsafe { bindings::irqs_disabled() });
> +
> +        Self(PhantomData)
This needs to assert INVARIANT.
> +    }

When you say assert do you just mean I should be saying something like "INVARIANT: This check ensures that interrupts are actually disabled"? I was hesistant to add that since -technically- it does, but seeing as it's a debug assertion that is only true during debug builds. Otherwise we're simply relying on the user following the safety contract

Gary Guo (Sep 16 2024 at 21:04):

It could just be INVARIANT: The safety requirements of this function ensure that IRQ is disabled

Lyude (Sep 16 2024 at 21:04):

oooh, ok

Lyude (Sep 16 2024 at 21:08):

@Gary Guo so then I assume for the documentation on IrqDisabled itself the invariants comment would look something like this?

/// # Invariants
///
/// The safety requirements of this function's constructor ([`IrqDisabled::new`]) ensure that IRQs are disabled while this type is in scope

Gary Guo (Sep 16 2024 at 21:13):

Invariant should encode some property that you can deduce from the an object. So I think something along the line of

/// # Invariants
///
/// IRQ is disabled when an object of this type exists.

would be better?

Lyude (Sep 16 2024 at 21:14):

Gotcha

Gary Guo (Sep 16 2024 at 21:14):

Then whenever you have to assert IRQ is disabled in a safety comment you can quote this invariant directly

Lyude (Sep 16 2024 at 21:24):

also, @Benno Lossin I think I'm just going to make the series depend on the NotThreadSafe marker. I didn't realize until just now, but git has a concept of a prerequisite-patch-id tag

Boqun Feng (Sep 17 2024 at 04:58):

(but shouldn't you use Alice's patch's URL as the depend? Currently, you use one of my emails ;-))

Andreas Hindborg (Oct 14 2024 at 09:28):

@Lyude do you want to hand CondVar::wait and friends for waiting with irq disabled, or should I draft up a patch for that? See here for discussion.

Boqun Feng (Oct 14 2024 at 15:41):

Careful, it might be more challenging than you thought ;-) And the changes are most likely need on lock side.

Andreas Hindborg (Oct 14 2024 at 15:52):

Boqun Feng said:

Careful, it might be more challenging than you thought ;-) And the changes are most likely need on lock side.

Not sure how to respond to that. It felt rude to as Lyude to do more work without offering to pitch in.

It would be great if we are able to add in the examples for hrtimer with the rest of the series.

Boqun Feng (Oct 14 2024 at 16:14):

So if I understand correctly what Lyude has proposed, we are going to have a lock_with_new() function for SpinLockIrq:

pub fn lock_with_new(&self, cb: impl FnOnce(&mut T, IrqDisabled<'a>) -> U) -> U

and with CondVar::wait in the picture, we need to support code below:

lock1.lock_with_new(|t, irq_disabled| {
    while *t != 5 {
        cv.wait(???); // no guard here.
    }
});

to make it work for CondVar::wait, we need at least 2 things:

  • making CondVar work without Guard, because lock_with_new doesn't have a Guard in its callback.
  • changing the IrqDisabled<'a> in the callback to a IrqDisabledOwned, so that the ownership of an interrupt disable state can be transfer to CondVar::wait and CondVar::wait could temporarily restore and re-save the state.

Boqun Feng (Oct 15 2024 at 21:19):

Correction, I think the lock_with_new() signature would be:

pub fn lock_with_new<U>(&self, cb: impl FnOnce(&mut Guard<'_, T, Backend>, IrqDisabled<'a>) -> U) -> U

Lyude (Oct 17 2024 at 20:52):

Andreas Hindborg said:

Lyude do you want to hand CondVar::wait and friends for waiting with irq disabled, or should I draft up a patch for that? See here for discussion.

mhh - might take me till early tomorrow - realized I completely forgot to rebase

Lyude (Oct 17 2024 at 22:07):

gotcha :P

Boqun Feng (Oct 17 2024 at 22:07):

btw, I keep the token type in the POC, but avoid closure API.

Boqun Feng (Oct 17 2024 at 22:09):

So basically with what I proposed for nested interrupt disable, SpinLockIrq now disable interrupts on its own, and it's safe to unlock in arbitrary order. So it can use the Guard api.

Boqun Feng (Oct 17 2024 at 22:09):

The remaining part is how to avoid disable interrupts if we know someone already disable the interrupts.

Boqun Feng (Oct 17 2024 at 22:11):

What I did is re-use the lock_with method, but give it a new semantics: A &SpinLockIrq can lock_with() a &InterruptDisabled context and return a Guard<.., *SpinLockBackend*>.

Boqun Feng (Oct 17 2024 at 22:12):

InterruptDisabled is the new owned interrupt disable token.

Boqun Feng (Oct 17 2024 at 22:20):

https://github.com/fbq/linux/tree/dev/nested-irq-disable

Boqun Feng (Oct 17 2024 at 22:21):

^ this is it, I still need to work on the hard pitch for the C changes.

Boqun Feng (Oct 17 2024 at 22:21):

And try with Andreas' hrtimer test case.

(updated with rustfmt fixes)

Lyude (Oct 21 2024 at 20:19):
