Boqun Feng (Oct 21 2024 at 20:22):

https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/b129cddc-862a-472b-b52a-2457b1a02d45@gmail.com/

Boqun Feng (Oct 21 2024 at 20:26):

Basically, there is a use case in timer, where the field is protected by a lock (should be a spin_lock in this case), timer callback can do "lock + modify + wakeup", a task context would do a "lock + check + sleep wait" loop.

Lyude (Oct 21 2024 at 20:45):

Gotcha. So - we -could- technically still support this with my API by introducing the owned variant of IrqDisabled.
But this being said I honestly like your solution a lot better. There's benefits to both the C and rust side of things, and it would let us easily have this all as safe code.

Boqun Feng (Oct 21 2024 at 20:53):

So there is a bridge can connect your design with what I have, we can define a 

pub struct IrqDisabledFlag(usize, NotThreadSafe);

whose new function is a unsafe one:

impl IrqDisabledFlag {
    /// Disable the interrupt and return the state.
    ///
    /// # Safety
    /// `drop` must be called with the same interrupt state as `new`.
    pub fn unsafe new() -> Self {
        let mut flags = 0;
        unsafe { local_irq_save(&mut flags); };
        Self(flags, NotThreadSafe)
    }
}

Usually this safety requirement would look weird, but we can actually check this at runtime:

impl Drop for IrqDisabledFlag {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        #[cfg(SOME_DEBUG)]
        {
            let mut flags = 0;
            unsafe { local_irq_save(&mut flags); }
            unsafe { local_irq_restore(flags); }
            assert_eq!(flags, self.0);
        }
        unsafe { local_irq_restore(self.0); }
    }
}

Boqun Feng (Oct 21 2024 at 20:54):

In this way, the correctness nesting is guaranteed by the safety requirement of IrqDisabledFlag::new().

Boqun Feng (Oct 21 2024 at 21:04):

And we can implement the InterruptDisabled in the POC with IrqDisabledFlags if we have PerCpu support in Rust:

pub struct InterruptDisabled(NotThreadSafe);

static IRQ_DISABLED_FLAG: PerCpu<Option<IrqDisabledFlags>> = PerCpu::new(None);
static IRQ_DISABLED_LEVEL: PerCpu<isize> = PerCpu::new(0);

impl InterruptDisabled {
    pub fn new() -> Self {
        let irq_disabled_flag = unsafe { IrqDisabledFlag::new() };
        if IRQ_DISABLE_LEVEL.inc_return() == 1 {
            (*IRQ_DISABLED_FLAG.deref_mut()) = Some(irq_disabled_flag);
        } else {
            forget(irq_disabled_flag);
        }
        Self(NotThreadSafe)
    }
}

impl Drop for InterruptDisabled {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        if IRQ_DISABLED_LEVEL.dec_return() == 0 {
            IRQ_DISABLED_FLAG.deref_mut().take(); // IrqDisabledFlag will drop and restore the state
        }
    }
}

Boqun Feng (Oct 21 2024 at 21:05):

After this, we will have more flexible interrupt control in Rust.

Boqun Feng (Oct 21 2024 at 21:06):

"if you really want to fine-grained control like C, you could use IrqDisabledFlags in the unsafe code"

Boqun Feng (Oct 21 2024 at 21:07):

(the PerCpu variable part is taken from a recent glance at what @Mitchell Levy is working on)

Boqun Feng (Oct 21 2024 at 21:09):

All the above seems to be a reasonable future plan for the "owned variant of IrqDisabled" from you.

Lyude (Oct 21 2024 at 21:15):

Mhm - though tbh, I'm kind of curious how much we really would need such an owned variant in general. The main reason we had it before was to deal with the Condvar pattern you described of "disable + check some condition + enable & sleep + disable (etc…)" while keeping the ability to have acquired resources tied to the lifetime of the interrupt state changes.
But I think as tglx pointed out we ideally should be trying to hide explicit enables/disables from the user, or at the very least make them a part of acquiring a resource so that developers are less encouraged to rely on this stuff unless they really know it's required. Though, if we were to implement some sort of locking primitives in the future (or maybe even for using in bindings for stuff like CondVar) this seems like a fairly reasonable idea.

Boqun Feng (Oct 21 2024 at 21:18):

Agreed! ;-)

Daniel Almeida (Feb 17 2025 at 12:03):

I guess you can just call this to get the token:

/// Disable interrupts.
pub fn interrupt_disable() -> InterruptDisabled {
    // SAFETY: It's always safe to call `local_interrupt_disable()`.
    unsafe { bindings::local_interrupt_disable() };

    InterruptDisabled(NotThreadSafe)
}

Still, if you guys are doing away with the closure approach from before, you should update the docs to make it clear

Lyude (Feb 20 2025 at 21:49):

gotcha, fwiw I don't think we'll need or want it - as tglx mentioned we want to make sure that interrupts get disabled for an explicit purpose along with an action that requires we do so (e.g. like how we've got spin_lock_irqsave() to use over just explicitly disabling interrupts).

Boqun Feng (Feb 20 2025 at 21:52):

Well, there will be code that needs to explicitly disable interrupt, i.e. we need it to be a building block of high level API

Lyude (Feb 20 2025 at 21:54):

gotcha. Also - I guess we're ok with using references for InterruptDisabled now instead of having a lifetime be part of the token type?

Boqun Feng (Feb 20 2025 at 21:58):

Yes, the idea is InterruptDisabled type representing a nested interrupt disabling critical section, so having one object of it means the interrupt is disabled and you can undo the disable by dropping it (which doesn't necessarily mean interrupt enabled, e.g. multiple InterruptDisabled may exist during interrupt disabling nesting).

Boqun Feng (Feb 20 2025 at 21:58):

so &'a InterruptDisabled is the old token type in your previous proposal.

Lyude (Feb 20 2025 at 23:22):

ok - I thiiiink this should work!

Boqun Feng (Feb 27 2025 at 00:20):

Oh I forgot to define the functions for UP:

diff --git a/include/linux/spinlock_api_up.h b/include/linux/spinlock_api_up.h
index 819aeba1c87e..d02a73671713 100644
--- a/include/linux/spinlock_api_up.h
+++ b/include/linux/spinlock_api_up.h
@@ -36,6 +36,9 @@
 #define __LOCK_IRQ(lock) \
   do { local_irq_disable(); __LOCK(lock); } while (0)

+#define __LOCK_IRQ_DISABLE(lock) \
+  do { local_interrupt_disable(); __LOCK(lock); } while (0)
+
 #define __LOCK_IRQSAVE(lock, flags) \
   do { local_irq_save(flags); __LOCK(lock); } while (0)

@@ -52,6 +55,9 @@
 #define __UNLOCK_IRQ(lock) \
   do { local_irq_enable(); __UNLOCK(lock); } while (0)

+#define __UNLOCK_IRQ_ENABLE(lock) \
+  do { __UNLOCK(lock); local_interrupt_enable(); } while (0)
+
 #define __UNLOCK_IRQRESTORE(lock, flags) \
   do { local_irq_restore(flags); __UNLOCK(lock); } while (0)

@@ -64,6 +70,7 @@
 #define _raw_read_lock_bh(lock)                        __LOCK_BH(lock)
 #define _raw_write_lock_bh(lock)               __LOCK_BH(lock)
 #define _raw_spin_lock_irq(lock)               __LOCK_IRQ(lock)
+#define _raw_spin_lock_irq_disable(lock)       __LOCK_IRQ_DISABLE(lock)
 #define _raw_read_lock_irq(lock)               __LOCK_IRQ(lock)
 #define _raw_write_lock_irq(lock)              __LOCK_IRQ(lock)
 #define _raw_spin_lock_irqsave(lock, flags)    __LOCK_IRQSAVE(lock, flags)
@@ -80,6 +87,7 @@
 #define _raw_write_unlock_bh(lock)             __UNLOCK_BH(lock)
 #define _raw_read_unlock_bh(lock)              __UNLOCK_BH(lock)
 #define _raw_spin_unlock_irq(lock)             __UNLOCK_IRQ(lock)
+#define _raw_spin_unlock_irq_enable(lock)      __UNLOCK_IRQ_ENABLE(lock)
 #define _raw_read_unlock_irq(lock)             __UNLOCK_IRQ(lock)
 #define _raw_write_unlock_irq(lock)            __UNLOCK_IRQ(lock)
 #define _raw_spin_unlock_irqrestore(lock, flags) \

Commit messages fixed up, I think I need to apply some of the C side changes from the mailing list still and do my best to see if I can figure out anything w/r/t to the performance regression that got mentioned

Boqun Feng (Apr 03 2025 at 23:13):

the type generic of GlobalGuard can be ‘_ in as_ref() and as_mut(), right?

Boqun Feng (Apr 03 2025 at 23:13):

it looks incorrect to be ‘a

Boqun Feng (Apr 03 2025 at 23:14):

but I’m only looking this from my phone

Lyude (Apr 03 2025 at 23:14):

oh can it be? I would have assumed it would have been 'a since we want it to match the lifetime of the Context right?

Boqun Feng (Apr 03 2025 at 23:15):

the Guard can outlive the returned references, no?

Boqun Feng (Apr 03 2025 at 23:15):

i.e. if it’s ’b then we have ’b : ‘a

Lyude (Apr 03 2025 at 23:17):

ooooo! yes ok, you're right

Lyude (Apr 04 2025 at 23:06):

@Boqun Feng I'm looking at the bug with __raw_spin_lock_irq_disable not being defined that the kernel test robot pointed out on the last version of the spinlock irq patch series and it looks like the reason is that we don't define this with BUILD_LOCK_OPS()… however: it seems that said macro doesn't just handle spin lock variants but also:

/*
 * Build preemption-friendly versions of the following
 * lock-spinning functions:
 *
 *         __[spin|read|write]_lock()
 *         __[spin|read|write]_lock_irq()
 *         __[spin|read|write]_lock_irqsave()
 *         __[spin|read|write]_lock_bh()
 */
BUILD_LOCK_OPS(spin, raw_spinlock);

…are we planning on ever adding read/write variants for this? I don't see any defined in the current patch set

Boqun Feng (Apr 04 2025 at 23:16):

We can just add the necessary bits

Boqun Feng (Apr 04 2025 at 23:17):

i.e. __raw_spin_lock_irq_disable(), no need to do it for rwlock_t now.

Daniel Almeida (Apr 07 2025 at 11:19):

@Lyude I think we've established in the last RFL meeting that I was using an earlier version, and that the newer one wouldn't have the issue I was complaining about

Daniel Almeida (Apr 07 2025 at 11:21):

The problem was that do_unlocked() went to sleep without re-enabling interrupts, as it did not consume the InterruptDisabled token

Lyude (Apr 08 2025 at 21:09):

ahhh gotcha

Lyude (Apr 30 2025 at 21:46):

@Boqun Feng I think I've got everything handled with this series except:

  • the performance issue
  • Peter's comment about going from 16 to 8 NMI IRQ levels.
    Regarding the second one - could we possibly avoid that by going from 256 max irq disables to 128?

Lyude (Apr 30 2025 at 21:52):

...also, what is an IST gate?

Lyude (Apr 30 2025 at 23:03):

also - sorry for all the questions, I'm also struggling a bit to figure out how to check if we're going to break any architectures with IPL. honestly, I'm having a really hard time understanding where the hardirq bitmask even lives on any architecture (I have extremely little experience with asm or any part of the kernel that does anything processor specific)

Boqun Feng (Apr 30 2025 at 23:04):

Lyude said:

Boqun Feng I think I've got everything handled with this series except:

  • the performance issue
  • Peter's comment about going from 16 to 8 NMI IRQ levels.
    Regarding the second one - could we possibly avoid that by going from 256 max irq disables to 128?

Regarding the second one, I think that's doable, however, we should be careful, maybe we want the extra bit from softirq instead of hardirq (depending on which part of preempt_count is less hot).

Boqun Feng (Apr 30 2025 at 23:08):

From: https://0xax.gitbooks.io/linux-insides/content/Interrupts/linux-interrupts-1.html

The IST or Interrupt Stack Table is a new mechanism in the x86_64. It is used as an alternative to the legacy stack-switch mechanism. Previously the x86 architecture provided a mechanism to automatically switch stack frames in response to an interrupt. The IST is a modified version of the x86 Stack switching mode. This mechanism unconditionally switches stacks when it is enabled and can be enabled for any interrupt in the IDT entry related with the certain interrupt (we will soon see it). From this we can understand that IST is not necessary for all interrupts. Some interrupts can continue to use the legacy stack switching mode. The IST mechanism provides up to seven IST pointers in the Task State Segment or TSS which is the special structure which contains information about a process. The TSS is used for stack switching during the execution of an interrupt or exception handler in the Linux kernel. Each pointer is referenced by an interrupt gate from the IDT.

Lyude (Apr 30 2025 at 23:22):

@Boqun Feng I guess I should ask, is there any chance you might have time soon to work on the performance issues/IPL questions if I got another version of this patch series onto the list?

Lyude said:

that would be hugely appreciated, if the performance stuff doesn't cause problems outside of rust code as well I wnder if maybe we should just focus on trying to get the patches merged first

If I understand Peter's concern correctly, he would like to see if we can kill all the usage of local_irq_disable() and spin_lock_irq(), so in an ideal world, we should have only two set of APIs:

  • _irq(), but has a different semantics than today's version, it uses the preempt_count to track irq disabling nested levels, and work for most cases and it's a safe API.
  • _irq_{save,restore}(), same semantics as today, but is only supposed to be used in the case where performance really masters or we want to play with unbalanced irq disable/enable, it's a unsafe API.

Lyude (May 05 2025 at 21:45):

BTW - https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/commits/rust/irq-spinlock current version of the series since I haven't sent it to the mail list quite yet

Lyude (May 28 2025 at 22:01):

@Boqun Feng trying to write something up for describing the other thing that we're missing in the implementation, does this sound right?

  • Figuring out if there is a notable performance impact from the conditional that would need to be added for overflow checking in functions like __preempt_count_add as described here: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/ZxrCrlg1XvaTtJ1I@boqun-archlinux/ (also, see the reply from thomas and peter)

Lyude (May 28 2025 at 22:01):

just want to make sure I understand this properly myself as well

Boqun Feng (May 28 2025 at 22:03):

Looks good to me! Thanks!

Lyude (May 28 2025 at 22:03):

np, will add it to the hackmd

Daniel Almeida (May 29 2025 at 14:50):

@Lyude @Boqun Feng

Hey there,

I didn't want to deviate the topic too much yesterday on the call, but we should make sure this series work with CondVar

Daniel Almeida (May 29 2025 at 14:51):

@Boqun Feng Boqun said this was fixed already, but I see that whatever type is being used to keep track of the level of enable/disable nesting is apparently getting reworked yet again

Daniel Almeida (May 29 2025 at 14:52):

It's very important to get CondVar working from the get go. A very frequent use-case is to wake up waiters when a device interrupt fires

Daniel Almeida (May 29 2025 at 14:56):

I can help out with testing, if needed

Boqun Feng (May 29 2025 at 14:58):

it should work, @Lyude maybe you can add a test for the future version, since we already have the #[kunit_tests()] macro

Lyude (May 29 2025 at 18:28):

@Daniel Almeida yeah I'm happy to take a look, are there any patch series in particular that I should be applying for this?

Lyude (May 29 2025 at 21:32):

@Daniel Almeida https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/commit/a4cfbe55b63387f235b5dd126ba16ac27443bd28 wrote up a test for the condvar stuff btw, does this look good to you? (also it does pass)

Daniel Almeida (Jun 02 2025 at 11:43):

Lyude said:

Daniel Almeida https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/commit/a4cfbe55b63387f235b5dd126ba16ac27443bd28 wrote up a test for the condvar stuff btw, does this look good to you? (also it does pass)

Hi Lyude, will try this later today

Lyude (Jun 03 2025 at 18:10):

@Daniel Almeida btw, realized I meant to link this commit instead oops https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/commit/80b6607fe2bb91f37c55be85b7221898ce2ef751

Daniel Almeida (Jun 03 2025 at 18:18):

Lyude said:

Daniel Almeida btw, realized I meant to link this commit instead oops https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/commit/80b6607fe2bb91f37c55be85b7221898ce2ef751

Lyude, I assume this can be tested through

 ./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --make_options LLVM=1 --arch x86_64 --kconfig_add CONFIG_RUST=y

Lyude (Jun 03 2025 at 18:18):

yep!

Daniel Almeida (Jun 03 2025 at 18:33):

@Lyude

The test LGTM, and it passes here, but I wonder whether there's a way to run this test with lockdep enabled?

Lyude (Jun 03 2025 at 18:34):

@Daniel Almeida what do you mean? I don't think I ever checked for lockdep here, unless you mean whether we can check for interrupt state violations without lockdep?

Daniel Almeida (Jun 03 2025 at 18:36):

The original problem was in this function:

    pub(crate) fn do_unlocked<U>(&mut self, cb: impl FnOnce() -> U) -> U {
        // SAFETY: The caller owns the lock, so it is safe to unlock it.
        unsafe { B::unlock(self.lock.state.get(), &self.state) };

        let _relock = ScopeGuard::new(||
                // SAFETY: The lock was just unlocked above and is being relocked now.
                unsafe { B::relock(self.lock.state.get(), &mut self.state) });

        cb()
    }

The lock would get dissociated from the InterruptDisabled token back then, so when the condvar called schedule_timeout, interrupts would still be disabled, thereby triggering a bug

Boqun Feng (Jun 03 2025 at 18:36):

you can enable LOCKDEP with the kconfig_add option of kuint

Daniel Almeida (Jun 03 2025 at 18:37):

IOW: do_unlocked() would release the lock and go to sleep, where the correct behavior would be to release the lock _and_ reenable interrupts

Boqun Feng (Jun 03 2025 at 18:38):

yes, that’s what the new version did

Lyude (Jun 03 2025 at 18:39):

Daniel Almeida said:

The original problem was in this function:

    pub(crate) fn do_unlocked<U>(&mut self, cb: impl FnOnce() -> U) -> U {
        // SAFETY: The caller owns the lock, so it is safe to unlock it.
        unsafe { B::unlock(self.lock.state.get(), &self.state) };

        let _relock = ScopeGuard::new(||
                // SAFETY: The lock was just unlocked above and is being relocked now.
                unsafe { B::relock(self.lock.state.get(), &mut self.state) });

        cb()
    }

The lock would get dissociated from the InterruptDisabled token back then, so when the condvar called schedule_timeout, interrupts would still be disabled, thereby triggering a bug

Sorry - I'm still a bit confused here, when you say "Back then" do you mean in an older version of the patch series?

Daniel Almeida (Jun 03 2025 at 18:40):

yes, in an older version, back when you guys had that "InterruptDisabled" token, before the idea of nesting was introduced (IIRC)

Lyude (Jun 03 2025 at 18:40):

ahh gotcha

Lyude (Jun 03 2025 at 18:41):

ok I'm following you now, so then it does seem like our best option is just to make kunit enable the lockdep option for this unittest

Lyude (Jun 03 2025 at 18:42):

btw @Boqun Feng do we actually have bindings in rust to use this part of kunit?

Boqun Feng (Jun 03 2025 at 18:42):

which “part” you mean?

Lyude (Jun 03 2025 at 18:43):

ah whoops - I mean the ability to set arbitrary kernel config options for a specific kunit test

Boqun Feng (Jun 03 2025 at 18:43):

Again, you can enable LOCKDEP with kunit option

Lyude (Jun 03 2025 at 18:43):

ooooh - ok i misunderstood

Lyude (Jun 03 2025 at 18:43):

for some reason I thought you meant that you needed to add something to the kunit attribute specifically, got it

Boqun Feng (Jun 03 2025 at 18:43):

no problem

Boqun Feng (Jun 03 2025 at 18:45):

besides, I think DEBUG_ATOMIC_SLEEP is supposed to catch the problem here, so maybe enable that as well

Daniel Almeida (Jun 03 2025 at 18:46):

ugh:

./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --make_options LLVM=1 --arch x86_64 --kconfig_add CONFIG_RUST=y --kconfig_add CONFIG_LOCKDEP=y --kconfig_add CONFIG_DEBUG_KERNEL=y  --kconfig_add CONFIG_LOCK_DEBUGGING_SUPPORT=y
[15:44:29] Configuring KUnit Kernel ...
Regenerating .config ...
Populating config with:
$ make ARCH=x86_64 O=.kunit olddefconfig LLVM=1
ERROR:root:Not all Kconfig options selected in kunitconfig were in the generated .config.
This is probably due to unsatisfied dependencies.
Missing: CONFIG_LOCKDEP=y

Daniel Almeida (Jun 03 2025 at 18:46):

does anybody spot anything missing?

Daniel Almeida (Jun 03 2025 at 18:47):

I don't think this thing actually resolves dependencies x_x

Lyude (Jun 03 2025 at 18:48):

FWIW I think that config options like that should be for enabling/disabling certain features in the kernel w/r/t kunit, if LOCKDEP is supposed to be enabled by default I take that as a sign that we don't want tests that can pass/fail depending on the kernel config

Boqun Feng (Jun 03 2025 at 18:49):

CONFIG_PROVING_LOCK=y maybe?

Lyude (Jun 03 2025 at 18:49):

though, maybe I'm missing something but where exactly does the KUNIT config option depend on CONFIG_LOCKDEP=y?

Boqun Feng (Jun 03 2025 at 18:49):

Lyude said:

FWIW I think that config options like that should be for enabling/disabling certain features in the kernel w/r/t kunit, if LOCKDEP is supposed to be enabled by default I take that as a sign that we don't want tests that can pass/fail depending on the kernel config

except that LOCKDEP is only a debug tool

Daniel Almeida (Jun 03 2025 at 18:51):

@Boqun Feng now both are missing:

./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --make_options LLVM=1 --arch x86_64 --kconfig_add CONFIG_RUST=y --kconfig_add CONFIG_LOCKDEP=y --kconfig_add CONFIG_DEBUG_KERNEL=y  --kconfig_add CONFIG_LOCK_DEBUGGING_SUPPORT=y --kconfig_add CONFIG_PROVING_LOCK=y

$ make ARCH=x86_64 O=.kunit olddefconfig LLVM=1

Lyude (Jun 03 2025 at 18:51):

Boqun Feng said:

Lyude said:

FWIW I think that config options like that should be for enabling/disabling certain features in the kernel w/r/t kunit, if LOCKDEP is supposed to be enabled by default I take that as a sign that we don't want tests that can pass/fail depending on the kernel config

except that LOCKDEP is only a debug tool

Do you mean that we want kunit tests to be able to run on non-debugging kernels?

Daniel Almeida (Jun 03 2025 at 18:51):

can't we change this through a menuconfig-like thing, where it automatically selects the dependencies?

Boqun Feng (Jun 03 2025 at 18:53):

Lyude said:

Boqun Feng said:

Lyude said:

FWIW I think that config options like that should be for enabling/disabling certain features in the kernel w/r/t kunit, if LOCKDEP is supposed to be enabled by default I take that as a sign that we don't want tests that can pass/fail depending on the kernel config

except that LOCKDEP is only a debug tool

Do you mean that we want kunit tests to be able to run on non-debugging kernels?

Yes, and that test case covers more that LOCKDEP=y scenario.

Boqun Feng (Jun 03 2025 at 18:53):

So I don't see a reason to make that test case depends on LOCKDEP=y

Boqun Feng (Jun 03 2025 at 18:57):

Daniel Almeida said:

can't we change this through a menuconfig-like thing, where it automatically selects the dependencies?

It's not hard to find it yourself, but here you go:

--kconfig_add CONFIG_DEBUG_KERNEL=y --kconfig_add CONFIG_PROVE_LOCKING=y --kconfig_add CONFIG_LOCKDEP=y

Boqun Feng (Jun 03 2025 at 18:57):

Need DEBUG_KERNEL, and it's PROVE_LOCKING

Lyude (Jun 04 2025 at 18:38):

btw - do we have anyone willing to volunteer for figuring out the impact from the overflow checks like what was mentioned here? https://hackmd.io/1jYFkNEzRka6vUtiKkLpYA?both

Boqun Feng (Jun 04 2025 at 18:39):

FWIW, I'm summarizing items needed, and should post them in a Github issue.

Lyude (Jun 04 2025 at 18:40):

Oh - I think I missed the part about filing them on github

Lyude (Jun 04 2025 at 18:46):

Would it get picked up within a reasonable time frame, though? It's just that this is kind of getting to be a high priority series for nova because we really need irq spinlocks. I'm also just kind of concerned because I was trying to point out before, the summary I put on the meeting notes -is- the extent of my understanding of the problem. I couldn't come up with a reasonable issue describing exactly what needs to be done beyond that, otherwise I would have probably just handled it myself at this point.

Boqun Feng (Jun 04 2025 at 19:05):

Yeah, this is a big project, we probably want to split items and see what we can do. So far the major ones are:

  • Performance evaluation of the changes, with these requirements:
    • Support the same number of NMI nesting level we have now.
    • Overflow detection for irq disable nest levels
    • One major impact is that hardware access vs per-CPU memory access at each time we disable/enable irq.
  • Plan/tool/work of avoiding introduction of another irq disable/enable primitives.

Boqun Feng (Jun 04 2025 at 19:06):

I think the second part is more important for the timeline imo

Lyude (Jun 04 2025 at 19:07):

yeah - I'm still fine with handling the second one I'm pretty sure btw, as long as we don't think the first item is going to cause problems getting this accepted upstream in the meantime

Lyude (Jun 04 2025 at 20:05):

Do we need to do anything about removing the local_irq_save() call from the local_interrupt_disable() (and vice versa for local_irq_restore)? Or will be good enough to just go through the kernel and replace the usecases of local_irq_save() with local_interrupt_disable()?

Boqun Feng (Jun 04 2025 at 20:09):

I think we only need to remove the public usage (for making forward progress of course, eventually we need to clean up things).

Lyude (Jun 05 2025 at 17:44):

@Boqun Feng gotcha, been working on a cocinelle script for replacing usages across the kernel (I assume that's what you meant by public usage)

Boqun Feng (Jun 05 2025 at 17:51):

What I meant is we can either:

  • keep the local_irq_disable() name, but implemented with the local_interrupt_disable(), or
  • Replace all local_irq_disable() with local_interrupt_disable().

Boqun Feng (Jun 05 2025 at 17:56):

So what we have current is:

  • local_irq_disable() and local_irq_enable()
  • local_irq_save() and local_irq_restore()

And what we are introducing is:

  • local_interrupt_disable() and local_interrupt_enable().

If we want to avoid 3 sets of API in total, the thing we should do is using local_interrupt_disable() to replace either local_irq_disable() or local_irq_save(). My gut feeling is that replacing local_irq_disable() will be easier.

Lyude (Jun 05 2025 at 17:56):

oh! wow ok I definitely misunderstood something, I didn't even notice we had both local_irq_disable and local_interrupt_disable until just now lol

Boqun Feng (Jun 05 2025 at 17:57):

local_irq_disable() expects the interrupt is enabled when it's called.

Lyude (Jun 05 2025 at 17:57):

and yeah - I think it will be because I just noticed one response that I somehow didn't see before:
https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20241024161759.GF9767@noisy.programming.kicks-ass.net/

Boqun Feng (Jun 05 2025 at 17:59):

right, what Peter did was replacing local_irq_save() with local_interrupt_disable(), which might look reasonable at the beginning, but I think the more appropriate thing to do is to replace local_irq_disable() with local_interrupt_disable().

Lyude (Jun 05 2025 at 17:59):

ahh gotcha

Boqun Feng (Jun 05 2025 at 17:59):

And I'm talking about semantically replacement

Boqun Feng (Jun 05 2025 at 17:59):

that is, we can keep the API name, but change the semantics.

Lyude (Jun 05 2025 at 18:00):

gotcha

Lyude (Jun 05 2025 at 20:40):

Starting to think that a conversion might actually be needed vs. reimplementation, or at the very least at the moment I'm actually having trouble actually getting kunit tests to run with this:
https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/commits/rust/irq-spinlock

Lyude (Jun 06 2025 at 17:33):

Oh. well :). I actually managed to just get a conversion working, apparently the only file that's not happy with the conversion is kernel/softirq.c

Lyude (Jun 06 2025 at 17:34):

at least for the kunit tests. would have to try booting this up on an actual VM to see if other things work

Lyude (Jun 06 2025 at 20:08):

oh yay, fwiw: turns out that kgdboc actually still works so I've finally got some backtraces from things not booting

Lyude (Jun 06 2025 at 22:14):

oh. Well ok then. I got it to boot

Lyude (Jun 06 2025 at 22:16):

Even looking at fbcon right now! How about that :)

Lyude (Jun 06 2025 at 22:17):

What I ended up doing was starting off with writing up a patch to convert all "balanced" calls, e.g. functions where there's both an interrupt enabled/disabled

Lyude (Jun 10 2025 at 22:23):

https://hackmd.io/gQ_du-9_R6636BEuKlw3ow
@Joel Fernandes btw ^ this gives a bit more information on the stuff I was talking about in the meeting. @Boqun Feng jfyi, joel is willing to spend time on this if you want someone to handle figuring out the performance impact/checking some of the other concerns that got brought up on the mailing list

Lyude (Jun 10 2025 at 22:23):

since nvidia is currently interested in getting this to work

Boqun Feng (Jun 10 2025 at 22:24):

sounds good :+1:

Lyude (Jul 16 2025 at 22:29):

Hey! Back working on this now that I'm finally back from PTO and dealing with other things. @Boqun Feng, I was wondering if @Joel Fernandes 's suggestion about waiting until a little later the conversion and letting people try it out before making the decision to convert everything to usin refcounted interrupts makes sense?

Boqun Feng (Jul 17 2025 at 19:25):

Do you have a link to Joel's suggestion?

Lyude (Jul 17 2025 at 19:26):

@Boqun Feng https://lkml.org/lkml/2025/6/16/1370

Boqun Feng (Jul 17 2025 at 19:26):

Yeah, I thought I replied that: https://lkml.org/lkml/2025/6/16/1377

Boqun Feng (Jul 17 2025 at 19:28):

i.e. we have to have evidences to prove the conversion is working, but we don't necessarily do the conversion at first.

Boqun Feng (Jul 17 2025 at 19:30):

Also I think Joel had patch using refscale for performance measurement, it's worth taking as well: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20250619175335.2905836-1-joelagnelf@nvidia.com/

Boqun Feng (Jul 17 2025 at 19:35):

Boqun Feng said:

i.e. we have to have evidences to prove the conversion is working, but we don't necessarily do the conversion at first.

I believe now we have two (kinda) evidences?

  1. You made some conversions and you can successfully boot the kernel: https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/commits/rust/irq-spinlock
  2. Joel refscale shows the number of the conversion impact on performance. (Do we have a number how much time it cost for a general irq-disable section? Maybe in some of your code?)

Boqun Feng (Jul 17 2025 at 19:37):

What we need to demonstrate to Peter is: "if we want to do the conversion, we can do it, and the performance impact is such such", that could resolve one of his concern.

Lyude (Jul 17 2025 at 19:38):

Boqun Feng said:

i.e. we have to have evidences to prove the conversion is working, but we don't necessarily do the conversion at first.

to be clear: -mostly-, I definitely had to be careful with the conversions I did since there's a few instances where things did break. but they were all things more complicated then grab a lock, drop it, etc.

Lyude (Jul 17 2025 at 19:39):

Boqun Feng said:

Also I think Joel had patch using refscale for performance measurement, it's worth taking as well: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20250619175335.2905836-1-joelagnelf@nvidia.com/

I'll try and take a look at this today

Boqun Feng (Jul 17 2025 at 19:40):

Lyude said:

Boqun Feng said:

i.e. we have to have evidences to prove the conversion is working, but we don't necessarily do the conversion at first.

to be clear: -mostly-, I definitely had to be careful with the conversions I did since there's a few instances where things did break. but they were all things more complicated then grab a lock, drop it, etc.

"Mostly" might be fine, we could present a list of things that would break if we do the conversion, it'll help make a conversion plan.

Boqun Feng (Jul 17 2025 at 19:41):

like: "if we do the conversion, function foo() breaks, because xyz, and to resolve that we can do abc". Having couples of these based on your experiment would be very helpful.

Boqun Feng (Jul 17 2025 at 19:42):

(but in general, I'm sorry I was caught up with other work, haven't spent much time on this one)

Lyude (Jul 17 2025 at 21:54):

phew, ~4ns local_irq, ~15ns local_interrupt

Boqun Feng (Jul 17 2025 at 21:55):

That's empty vs empty, right?

Lyude (Jul 17 2025 at 21:55):

Not sure I understand what you're asking?

Lyude (Jul 17 2025 at 22:03):

oh - do you mean like enable ←→ disable?

Lyude (Jul 17 2025 at 22:03):

e.g. when the disable count goes from 1 to 0 and vice versa

Boqun Feng (Jul 17 2025 at 22:03):

Yes, I meant there is nothing in the critical section.

Boqun Feng (Jul 17 2025 at 22:07):

local_irq_disable();
// <- nothing here
local_irq_enable();

Lyude (Jul 17 2025 at 22:07):

@Boqun Feng gotcha. And, mostly, but it seems like half of the test does have a short thing in the critical section:

        local_interrupt_disable();
        un_delay(udl, ndl);
        local_interrupt_enable();

(ref_local_interrupt_delay_section)

Boqun Feng (Jul 17 2025 at 22:09):

Yes, I think that's controlled by a model parameter (whether you want to have a delay in your critical section)

Lyude (Jul 17 2025 at 22:20):

Hm. I -did- just notice though, __preempt_count appears to use DECLARE_PER_CPU_CACHE_HOT. running a build now to see if using that here for local_interrupt_state makes any difference

Lyude (Jul 18 2025 at 19:04):

BTW - did we ever have any ideas for making this faster? Mainly wondering if there's stuff I should be trying to improve these benchmarks vs. just limiting this to rust for the time being.

Boqun Feng (Jul 18 2025 at 19:06):

Lyude said:

BTW - did we ever have any ideas for making this faster?

No, we probably are using the fastest version I believe.

Lyude (Jul 18 2025 at 19:08):

I guess then my question is if we want to do any more testing, or if I should just go ahead and mention this on the patch series and figure out what we want to do there

Lyude (Jul 18 2025 at 19:10):

Boqun Feng (Jul 18 2025 at 19:10):

Ah, I see

Lyude (Jul 18 2025 at 19:10):

Lyude (Jul 18 2025 at 19:11):

Lyude (Jul 18 2025 at 19:36):

right, please give me some time to compile stuff

Joel Fernandes (Jul 18 2025 at 19:40):

Boqun Feng (Jul 18 2025 at 19:41):

Daniel Almeida said:

do I need to do any other setup, or is it about just compiling the branch and running that command?

If you need to run the particular case, you need extra parameters, let me find that.

Boqun Feng (Jul 18 2025 at 19:42):

Lyude said:

also oh i didn't realize you could run the test like that

That's just an easy way to run the refscale in a VM.

Boqun Feng (Jul 18 2025 at 19:44):

but @Daniel Almeida , you can also compile the kernel, install the kernel, and compile the refscale module (at kernel/rcu/refscale.c) and

modprobe refscale scale_type=local_irq <- this is for the base line
modprobe refscale scale_type=local_interrupt <- this is for the new API

Daniel Almeida (Jul 18 2025 at 19:48):

wait, modprobe the module, then what?

Daniel Almeida (Jul 18 2025 at 19:48):

then this? ./tools/testing/sefltests/rcutorture/bin/kvm.sh --torture refscale

Boqun Feng (Jul 18 2025 at 19:49):

Boqun Feng said:

Daniel Almeida said:

do I need to do any other setup, or is it about just compiling the branch and running that command?

If you need to run the particular case, you need extra parameters, let me find that.

./tools/testing/sefltests/rcutorture/bin/kvm.sh --torture refscale --bootargs "rcuscale.scale_type=local_irq"

Boqun Feng (Jul 18 2025 at 19:49):

Daniel Almeida said:

then this? ./tools/testing/sefltests/rcutorture/bin/kvm.sh --torture refscale

Nope, modprobe will get the module run

Daniel Almeida (Jul 18 2025 at 19:50):

so there's the kernel running on the device is unaffected, right? it will compile a kernel and then boot that in a VM

Boqun Feng (Jul 18 2025 at 19:50):

Either you modprobe the module, which would run the benchmark at your current kernel, or you use that command to use qemu run the benchmark in a VM

Boqun Feng (Jul 18 2025 at 19:51):

Daniel Almeida said:

so there's the kernel running on the device is unaffected, right? it will compile a kernel and then boot that in a VM

Right, if you use the scripts, then it's all in a VM.

Lyude (Jul 18 2025 at 19:53):

so keep in mind i'm not sure that command runs both local_irq and local_interrupt

Lyude (Jul 18 2025 at 19:53):

it seems like it just ran the former for me

Boqun Feng (Jul 18 2025 at 19:55):

Lyude said:

so keep in mind i'm not sure that command runs both local_irq and local_interrupt

Yeah, if you use the script, you will need to run 

./tools/testing/sefltests/rcutorture/bin/kvm.sh --torture refscale --bootargs "rcuscale.scale_type=local_irq"

for the baseline, and

./tools/testing/sefltests/rcutorture/bin/kvm.sh --torture refscale --bootargs "rcuscale.scale_type=local_interrupt"

for the new API

Daniel Almeida (Jul 18 2025 at 20:15):

right, I'll get back in a couple of hours with results, I am still cloning the kernel and I'll then build, these things take a bit of time through NFS

Alright, Monday, let's get back to this ^

Boqun Feng (Jul 21 2025 at 16:14):

… in the “Results directory” mentioned above

Joel Fernandes (Jul 21 2025 at 16:14):

But the full build output should also be there in a Make* something file.

Daniel Almeida (Jul 21 2025 at 16:18):

@Boqun Feng This is the first place I looked at :)

I suggest the same.

Daniel Almeida (Jul 21 2025 at 16:22):

Right...let's try this then ^

Lyude (Jul 21 2025 at 17:12):

btw @Daniel Almeida how did you check your system has KVM support? I've got a bunch of arm boards lying around from when I worked on panfrost, been kinda curious about putting them to use since it couldn't hurt for me to have an arm platform for reference anyhow. one I'm currently looking at running is this guy https://libre.computer/products/aml-s905d3-cc/

Daniel Almeida (Jul 21 2025 at 17:18):

baseline: https://pastebin.com/3knLMmvP

Daniel Almeida (Jul 21 2025 at 17:25):

new api: https://pastebin.com/QwtKzjGw

Lyude (Jul 21 2025 at 17:25):

!! they look almost identical on yours

Daniel Almeida (Jul 21 2025 at 17:26):

@lyude I googled it and found people relating that they were running this promox thing on rk3588, so I looked on their website and found:

Use KVM full virtualization for Windows and Linux images, and lightweight containers to run conflict-free Linux applications.

Daniel Almeida (Jul 21 2025 at 17:36):

btw do you guys need anything else, or is what I sent enough to unblock you?

Lyude (Jul 21 2025 at 21:15):

Daniel Almeida said:

btw do you guys need anything else, or is what I sent enough to unblock you?

I think it's good! honestly atm I'm waiting to see if I get any responses on the current patch series

Lyude (Jul 21 2025 at 21:15):

it's really interesting to me that arm doesn't seem to be affected much at all by this

Joel Fernandes (Jul 22 2025 at 00:55):

Actually even on x86 I didn't think it was that effected. The latency hit is not even that of a dram cache miss. Plus consider the latency of spinlock contention which is much higher. I am curious how are we currently doing irq-disabled spinlocks in rust; in the absence of these patches?

Lyude (Jul 22 2025 at 00:59):

We aren't, except for C APIs we're calling down to that might use such spinlocks internally. Earlier versions of my spinlockirq patches used a closure interface instead to ensure that interrupt enable/disable was scoped. It worked fine with the current flags interface but it's a lot less convenient in situations where you have to potentially enable interrupts

Lyude (Jul 22 2025 at 00:59):

Like e.g. things that disable interrupts, grab a spin lock, check a condition and then enable interrupts and sleep on something

Joel Fernandes (Jul 22 2025 at 01:00):

Interesting. How common is a pattern like that?

Joel Fernandes (Jul 22 2025 at 01:01):

But yeah I hope this series gets some traction.

Lyude (Jul 22 2025 at 01:01):

Definitely not too uncommon, usually Condvar is the big one

Joel Fernandes (Jul 22 2025 at 01:01):

Ah yeah true.

Lyude (Jul 22 2025 at 01:02):

It was possible to do stuff like this with the closure interface but then you needed to introduce safety contracts regarding the state of interrupts that got quite complex

Lyude (Jul 22 2025 at 01:03):

whereas just having the locks be RAII lets us avoid unsafe code entirely, and has the benefit of being a lot less error prone then the current APIs on the C side

Joel Fernandes (Jul 22 2025 at 01:05):

Why not have both interfaces though

Joel Fernandes (Jul 22 2025 at 01:05):

It doesn't have to satisfy all use cases?

Joel Fernandes (Jul 22 2025 at 01:06):

Can be incremental

Lyude (Jul 22 2025 at 01:11):

Well the closures only really were introduced as a way to work around the problem of trying to just call spin_lock_irqsave/restore on the rust side in ::lock() and Drop using rust and not modifying the irqflags interface in the kernel. That problem being that if you dropped the locks in a different order then you acquired them and interrupts were only disabled on the first lock acquisition, you might enable interrupts spontaneously before releasing all the locks depending on them.

Joel Fernandes (Jul 22 2025 at 01:16):

Got it, I need to take time to review it more but makes sense

Boqun Feng (Jul 22 2025 at 01:19):

Joel Fernandes said:

Why not have both interfaces though

Well, you still can have what the closure API provided with the current API.

Joel Fernandes (Jul 22 2025 at 01:20):

That's true. It sounded like the old proposal was less contentious, hence the comment

Boqun Feng (Jul 22 2025 at 01:21):

with lock_with(), https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20250717191719.2076270-10-lyude@redhat.com/, you can get a &SpinLock from a &SpinLockIrq + &LocalInterruptDisabled.

Boqun Feng (Jul 22 2025 at 01:23):

So you could do:

let interrupt_guard = local_interrupt_disable();

let g1 = lock1.lock_with(&interrupt_guard);
let g2 = lock2.lock_with(&interrupt_guard);

^ you only need to disable interrupts once.

Boqun Feng (Jul 22 2025 at 01:25):

The reason that we absolutely need RAII-style of irq disabling/enabling is because we want SpinLockIrq to work with CondVar::wait(), i.e. you have to enable irq before go to sleep.

Boqun Feng (Jul 22 2025 at 01:29):

Lyude said:

Daniel Almeida said:

btw do you guys need anything else, or is what I sent enough to unblock you?

I think it's good! honestly atm I'm waiting to see if I get any responses on the current patch series

Yes, thanks a lot @Daniel Almeida ! IIUC the TODOs on the patchset are:

Boqun Feng (Jul 22 2025 at 01:35):

/todo TODOs
Understand the performance impact.
Have a plan for merge 3 APIs into 2, and some evidences to prove that's feasible.
Still support 16 level of NMI nesting
(can be a future work) Add overflow detection.

Lyude (Jul 22 2025 at 16:12):

So for the merging plan, I guess I can just respond to the patch series linking to some of the coccinelle scripts I've been running so far?
FWIW: can look into figuring out if we can detect and filter more problematic usecases of save/restore variants (like ones where we get the irq flags as a parameter from a function for instance)

Lyude (Jul 24 2025 at 18:14):

@Joel Fernandes btw - any chance you can check out the 16 level of NMI nesting detail if you've got any time?

Joel Fernandes (Jul 24 2025 at 18:14):

What does 16 level mean?

Lyude (Jul 24 2025 at 18:15):

My assumption is it has to do with the fact that since NMIs aren't interruptable, I assume then it's possible for us to end up within multiple nested NMI handlers

Lyude (Jul 24 2025 at 18:15):

e.g. you get an NMI while handling another NMI

Joel Fernandes (Jul 24 2025 at 20:19):

Lyude, I’m happy to look at it, but could you provide more context? is there a thread about it? As far as I know - NMIs are not safe places to use IRQ disabled spin locks.

Joel Fernandes (Jul 24 2025 at 20:20):

@Boqun Feng any pointers to help me out?

Boqun Feng (Jul 24 2025 at 20:22):

See Peter's reply at v9: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20250228075755.GC5880@noisy.programming.kicks-ass.net/

Lyude (Jul 24 2025 at 20:22):

btw @Boqun Feng

Lyude said:

So for the merging plan, I guess I can just respond to the patch series linking to some of the coccinelle scripts I've been running so far?
FWIW: can look into figuring out if we can detect and filter more problematic usecases of save/restore variants (like ones where we get the irq flags as a parameter from a function for instance)

does this sound fine to you?

Joel Fernandes (Jul 24 2025 at 20:23):

Oh Hehe I'm clearly out of shape because for some reason I really thought Linux didn't support nested NMIs :sob: :sob:

Joel Fernandes (Jul 24 2025 at 20:23):

I know we handle it in RCU but…

Lyude (Jul 24 2025 at 20:24):

I assume it's inevitable since we can't really mask a non-maskable interrupt

Joel Fernandes (Jul 24 2025 at 20:24):

Right

Boqun Feng (Jul 24 2025 at 20:24):

Lyude said:

So for the merging plan, I guess I can just respond to the patch series linking to some of the coccinelle scripts I've been running so far?
FWIW: can look into figuring out if we can detect and filter more problematic usecases of save/restore variants (like ones where we get the irq flags as a parameter from a function for instance)

does this sound fine to you?

It doesn't looks like a complete plan to me: it's more of an evidence.

Lyude (Jul 24 2025 at 20:25):

though. Maybe there's something else here I'm not aware of because I've never actually dealt with an NMI before (do some arches avoid providing nested NMIs to OSs?)

Joel Fernandes (Jul 24 2025 at 20:26):

Some arches don't have NMIs at all

Joel Fernandes (Jul 24 2025 at 20:27):

I think on x86 you have things like MCE which can interrupt NMI and that's where it comes from (could be wrong but that's my understanding )

Boqun Feng (Jul 24 2025 at 20:27):

A plan requires a few things:

  • What are we going to do (I think right now the target is to replace local_irq_disable() with local_interrupt_disable()).
  • What problems will we face?
  • How are we going to handle those problems? // <- the coccinelle scripts belong to this part
  • How will we be sure all the problems will be resolved.
  • A rough timeline of all the things that we are going to do.

Boqun Feng (Jul 24 2025 at 20:28):

Boqun Feng said:

See Peter's reply at v9: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20250228075755.GC5880@noisy.programming.kicks-ass.net/

I think for this particular request, we will have to use some of the SOFTIRQ_MASK bits as the 1 bits of NMI mask.

Joel Fernandes (Jul 24 2025 at 20:29):

So it appears Peter does feel such a high degree of nesting is achievable. Do we really need 256 levels of irq disable tracking? How many levels do we see in practice?

Lyude (Jul 24 2025 at 20:29):

i also wonder if we actually care about that for softirqs specifically

Lyude (Jul 24 2025 at 20:30):

hardirqs maybe I can see some evil

Boqun Feng (Jul 24 2025 at 20:31):

Joel Fernandes said:

So it appears Peter does feel such a high degree of nesting is achievable. Do we really need 256 levels of irq disable tracking? How many levels do we see in practice?

Peter was worried about preempt count overflow: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20241023195152.GE11151@noisy.programming.kicks-ass.net/

Lyude (Jul 24 2025 at 20:31):

"We still have a few holes in pcpu_hot iirc."
hm

Lyude (Jul 24 2025 at 20:32):

Do we know where these holes are? I wasn't able to get away with adding a single unsigned long when I tried

Boqun Feng (Jul 24 2025 at 20:33):

(need to AFK for a while).

Lyude (Jul 24 2025 at 20:36):

poked peter about the holes btw. Honestly that would probably let us address some of these problems if we can make use of them

Boqun Feng (Jul 24 2025 at 20:39):

Not all the archs have pcpu_hot

Lyude (Jul 24 2025 at 20:39):

in that case though I assume all the pcpu_hot stuff is just stored per-cpu without any special handling then?

Lyude (Jul 24 2025 at 20:40):

or is the storage literally not available on some arches

Boqun Feng (Jul 24 2025 at 20:41):

IIUC, it’s a special cacheline size percpu area where preempt_count is at.

Joel Fernandes (Jul 25 2025 at 19:36):

Is there something fundamentally broken about leaving the nesting counter as a separate counter? Preemption count was anyway independent of IRQ disabling before. Or was this attempted and did not work for some reason?

Lyude (Jul 25 2025 at 19:47):

Honestly I'm not actually sure, I know that it was brought up at some point during review but it might not be a bad idea to see if making the counter separate actually makes any difference with performance. since my assumption is that it was probably assumed to be faster since the preemption counter is in pcpu_hot

Boqun Feng (Jul 25 2025 at 19:52):

Joel Fernandes said:

Is there something fundamentally broken about leaving the nesting counter as a separate counter? Preemption count was anyway independent of IRQ disabling before. Or was this attempted and did not work for some reason?

Normally you will still need to check the nesting counter in preempt_enable() even if it's a separate counter, right?

There may be functions shared between NMI contexts and normal context, but you should not preempt in NMI contexts.

Boqun Feng (Jul 25 2025 at 19:54):

I think we could do is using a separate counter for nesting, but still keep bit to indicate it's in NMI context.

Boqun Feng (Jul 25 2025 at 19:56):

That would require you to check that bit every time in the NMI handler.

Lyude (Jul 25 2025 at 19:56):

btw - according to thomas there is 5 bytes left for pcpu_hot

Lyude (Jul 25 2025 at 19:58):

and I would certainly assume that 4 bytes is plenty for a counter like this

Lyude (Jul 25 2025 at 19:58):

maybe even 2

Boqun Feng (Jul 25 2025 at 19:58):

For irq-disabling, we actually have to use the preempt_count instead of other pcpu_hot, because that allow us to remove one preempt_disable() in spin_lock_interrupt_disable().

Boqun Feng (Jul 25 2025 at 19:59):

So if we were to use the other pcpu_hot area, it should be for the nesting counter of NMI.

Boqun Feng (Jul 25 2025 at 20:00):

At least it's more a hotpath for me ;-)

It might be a hotpath for perf events though.

Joel Fernandes (Jul 25 2025 at 20:45):

Normally you will still need to check the nesting counter in preempt_enable() even if it's a separate counter, right?

Don't we already do this check by checking the real IRQ flags from the CPU? AFAIK, we will not call schedule() from preempt_enable() unless the IRQ state is enabled

Joel Fernandes (Jul 25 2025 at 20:48):

I think we could do is using a separate counter for nesting, but still keep bit to indicate it's in NMI context.

That'd work for me, if it means progress ;-)

Boqun Feng (Jul 25 2025 at 21:47):

Joel Fernandes said:

Normally you will still need to check the nesting counter in preempt_enable() even if it's a separate counter, right?

Don't we already do this check by checking the real IRQ flags from the CPU? AFAIK, we will not call schedule() from preempt_enable() unless the IRQ state is enabled

You're right. Seems the only reason that we track NMI in preempt_count is for in_nmi() to work.

Joel Fernandes (Jul 25 2025 at 22:42):

Lyude said:

Honestly I'm not actually sure, I know that it was brought up at some point during review but it might not be a bad idea to see if making the counter separate actually makes any difference with performance. since my assumption is that it was probably assumed to be faster since the preemption counter is in pcpu_hot

We/I could make this change and test with refscale. Does that sound good? But for me it will have to be next Monday due to the weekend.

Lyude (Jul 26 2025 at 00:19):

Fine by me!

Lyude (Jul 28 2025 at 23:02):

@Boqun Feng been thinking about this a bit - do we think the expectation is that I/whoever working on this patch series is going to be responsible for converting the whole kernel over time? I know that the patch submission won't be blocked on that, I'm just considering the actual time requirements this all is going to take in the future

Lyude (Jul 28 2025 at 23:11):

i'm starting to really think it would be so much better if we could just, limit this to rust for the time being and let people in the kernel actually interested in making this work for their usecases just move over to the new function.

Lyude (Jul 28 2025 at 23:12):

still trying to write up a plan with the conversion btw.

Boqun Feng (Jul 28 2025 at 23:14):

Lyude said:

Boqun Feng been thinking about this a bit - do we think the expectation is that I/whoever working on this patch series is going to be responsible for converting the whole kernel over time? I know that the patch submission won't be blocked on that, I'm just considering the actual time requirements this all is going to take in the future

Yes, it's sort of our responsibility. Otherwise we may end up having 10+ irq disabling functions that don't work with each other ;-)

Lyude (Jul 28 2025 at 23:16):

even if we just say it's limited to rust, and the only interrupt enable/disabled allowed in rust code?

Boqun Feng (Jul 28 2025 at 23:18):

Well, that's not different than "we have this core kernel facility but only my driver (or drm driver) will use it."

Boqun Feng (Jul 28 2025 at 23:20):

You/we are changing the very core part of kernel, that's our responsibility to maintain everything still works, and that includes trying to reduce the numbers of "public" functions, i.e. reduce the maintenance burden from we get in our patches.

Lyude (Jul 28 2025 at 23:24):

yeah, guess I was mostly just asking since the earlier versions that I actually wrote - despite the interface not being as nice - didn't have anywhere this amount of long term commitment.

Boqun Feng (Jul 28 2025 at 23:25):

I agree, but now I think if we see it through, we get something better ;-)

Boqun Feng (Jul 28 2025 at 23:25):

Sorry I had to drag you into this huge amount of work :-)

Boqun Feng (Jul 28 2025 at 23:27):

That's why I think a LPC/Kangrejos talk may help, because we are essentially doing something impactful.

Lyude (Jul 28 2025 at 23:30):

hopefully i'll be able to balance this with the work i've been planning on doing for over a year now that this has all been prep work for: writing nova's display driver. Just since that is my priority here

Lyude (Jul 28 2025 at 23:31):

and isn't really going to change

Boqun Feng (Jul 28 2025 at 23:32):

I know, Joel and I will help you.

Boqun Feng (Jul 28 2025 at 23:33):

We all know there is a design gap in the beginning of Rust-for-Linux, it's only until recently we realized a "fix" can be done at C side.

Boqun Feng (Jul 28 2025 at 23:34):

Just let me know the exact changes you needed to make for booting, I could help on the plan as well.

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 14:30):

writing nova's display driver

Hmm. You do have some time for that since it is at least a few months before nova-core is able to provide nova-drm with services (in the upstream kernel). So feel free to priortize the interrupt work first if you would like.

I do agree with Boqun on seeing this through. Looking forward to any plan document you will share @Lyude and to pitch in where I can. I am going to research the "separate counter" idea today..

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 17:30):

gotcha. also thanks for the support! sorry haha, was definitely feeling a little burnt out about this last night but am feeling better now :P

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 17:31):

I'll send what I've got for getting the kernel to boot in just a bit

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:31):

been there, I have felt that over the years with many patches :P

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:31):

AFAICS, Lyude's POC was already trying the 'separate counter trick': 

+static inline void local_interrupt_disable(void)
+{
+   unsigned long flags;
+   long new_count;
+
+   local_irq_save(flags);
+
+   new_count = raw_cpu_inc_return(local_interrupt_disable_state.count);

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 17:32):

(full disclosure I think it was boqun who wrote that one :P)
also - that looks much different then the function that I've got in my tree?

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 17:33):

It's not ideal to put irq disabling counter in a separate coutner.

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 17:33):

DECLARE_PER_CPU(struct interrupt_disable_state, local_interrupt_disable_state);

static inline void local_interrupt_disable(void)
{
    unsigned long flags;
    int new_count;

    new_count = hardirq_disable_enter();

    if ((new_count & HARDIRQ_DISABLE_MASK) == HARDIRQ_DISABLE_OFFSET) {
        local_irq_save(flags);
        raw_cpu_write(local_interrupt_disable_state.flags, flags);
    }
}

static inline void local_interrupt_enable(void)
{
    int new_count;

    new_count = hardirq_disable_exit();

    if ((new_count & HARDIRQ_DISABLE_MASK) == 0) {
        unsigned long flags;

        flags = raw_cpu_read(local_interrupt_disable_state.flags);
        local_irq_restore(flags);
        /*
         * TODO: re-read preempt count can be avoided, but it needs
         * should_resched() taking another parameter as the current
         * preempt count
         */
#ifdef PREEMPTION
        if (should_resched(0))
            __preempt_schedule();
#endif
    }
}

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 17:33):

because you want to trigger preemption right at irq reenabling

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:34):

oh yeah it was Boqun: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20241018055125.2784186-1-boqun.feng@gmail.com/#t

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:36):

oh I see

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:37):

But in 'current code', we don't trigger preemption right after IRQ enabling I think.

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:37):

Hence the rcu_read_unlock_special() hacks :)

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 17:39):

by 'current code' you mean Lyude's latests patchset?

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:39):

no I mean upstream / mainline

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:39):

local_irq_enable() does not currently do that anyway right?

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 17:40):

They do when you spin_unlock_irq{restore}()

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:40):

that's because of the preempt_enable() in spinlock right? not solely because of local_irq_enable()

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:41):

preempt_disable/enable should still be done by spinlock, regardless of these patches , afaics

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 17:41):

Our proposed changes have the benefits of putting the hard irq disabling as part of the preempt_count

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:41):

Understood but it aggravates the running out of bits issue

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 17:42):

Yeah, so just use one bit for NMI, and use a separate field for nmi nesting

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 17:43):

I.e. we don't need to move the hard irq disabling bits out, but rather the NMI nesting bits

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:43):

sure, I think that will work. Has the idea been discussed upstream and agreed upon?

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 17:43):

Nope

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:43):

I see

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 17:44):

Do you want me to reply, Peter is OOO though

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:44):

yeah I think reply for sure! And CC Steven Rostedt if that's Ok, he's interested and I just talked to him about this last week.

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:44):

Steven is in office

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:45):

You'd have to handle the case where an NMI is received after the preempt_count is set, but before the separate NMI counter is incremented?

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:46):

Same problem in RCU handled by dynticks counters (+1 +2 dance)

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 17:46):

Joel Fernandes said:

You'd have to handle the case where an NMI is received after the preempt_count is set, but before the separate NMI counter is incremented?

Yep

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 17:46):

But it's NMI, it's weird anyway :)

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:46):

hehe..

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 17:48):

one sort of approach, is, I guess simply to check the per-cpu counters in context tracking (I think that also tracks NMI-nesting), and mirror that in case nested NMIs are received in the scenario I describe above. Another might be, to copy the logic from context tracking.

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 17:57):

Something like below should work?

--- a/include/linux/hardirq.h
+++ b/include/linux/hardirq.h
@@ -109,7 +109,10 @@ void irq_exit_rcu(void);
                lockdep_off();                                  \
                arch_nmi_enter();                               \
                BUG_ON(in_nmi() == NMI_MASK);                   \
-               __preempt_count_add(NMI_OFFSET + HARDIRQ_OFFSET);       \
+               this_cpu_inc(nmi_nesting);                      \
+               __preempt_count_add(HARDIRQ_OFFSET);            \
+               if (!in_mni())                                  \
+                       __preempt_count_add(NMI_OFFSET);        \
        } while (0)

 #define nmi_enter()                                            \
@@ -124,8 +127,12 @@ void irq_exit_rcu(void);

 #define __nmi_exit()                                           \
        do {                                                    \
+               int ret;                                        \
                BUG_ON(!in_nmi());                              \
-               __preempt_count_sub(NMI_OFFSET + HARDIRQ_OFFSET);       \
+               __preempt_count_sub(HARDIRQ_OFFSET);            \
+               ret = this_cpu_dec_return(nmi_nesting);         \
+               if (!ret)                                       \
+                       __preempt_count_sub(NMI_OFFSET);        \
                arch_nmi_exit();                                \
                lockdep_on();                                   \
        } while (0)

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 17:58):

nmi_nesting is a percpu in the cache hot zone.

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 18:01):

I am not confident this will will work because the nmi_nesting increment can get interrupted causing the lost-update-problem I think. So the counter can be clobbered due to interruption. Paul and Andy Luto's algorithm for context-tracking kind of solved that

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 18:01):

Joel Fernandes said:

Same problem in RCU handled by dynticks counters (+1 +2 dance)

Probably we don't need to do that because NMI nesting is local to the CPU, whereas dynticks needs to be read from remote CPUs.

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 18:02):

@Boqun Feng yeah true. Maybe it'll work. :)

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 18:03):

I think it might work because the current NMI nesting is similar, but good to use extra eyes for checking for sure!

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 18:04):

yeah the dynticks problem was slightly different I guess, it wasn't as much about counter corruption, but rather detecting the outer most NMI to report quiescent states

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 18:05):

https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/commit/8de13a2fdfa80a70e8e123a5f744b3b49aaa3971 btw - this is the latest conversion of local_irq_disable()/local_irq_enable() that I've done with the semantic patch included in the commit message which is:

// Options: --include-headers --allow-inconsistent-paths

virtual patch
virtual report

@depends on patch && !report@
@@
- local_irq_disable();
+ local_interrupt_disable();
...
- local_irq_enable();
+ local_interrupt_enable();

The reason I wrote the rule that way is because it seems pretty much all of the problematic areas I can find with this either only have "unbalanced" (e.g. only enable or disable interrupts, not both) uses of these functions, or areas where this sequence happens in reverse (like handle_softirq, which I assume is a problem due to prior expectations of what the interrupt state should be)

Joel Fernandes (Jul 29 2025 at 18:05):

One parallel is, in this case, we have the call schedule() and trigger the preempt count reset only from the other most NMI. I'll look into it more..

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 18:10):

I am thinking that it's possible there might be some scenarios where we may just need to unconditionally enable interrupts and ignore the reference counter, I'm assuming it's mostly situations where the interrupt state might be set by the processor and not by us

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 18:11):

Lyude said:

https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/commit/8de13a2fdfa80a70e8e123a5f744b3b49aaa3971 btw - this is the latest conversion of local_irq_disable()/local_irq_enable() that I've done with the semantic patch included in the commit message which is:

// Options: --include-headers --allow-inconsistent-paths

virtual patch
virtual report

@depends on patch && !report@
@@
- local_irq_disable();
+ local_interrupt_disable();
...
- local_irq_enable();
+ local_interrupt_enable();

The reason I wrote the rule that way is because it seems pretty much all of the problematic areas I can find with this either only have "unbalanced" (e.g. only enable or disable interrupts, not both) uses of these functions, or areas where this sequence happens in reverse (like handle_softirq, which I assume is a problem due to prior expectations of what the interrupt state should be)

Ok, but what if do just define local_irq_disable() using local_interrupt_disable() and similar for *enable()?

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 18:20):

and, weird question but - is there a function similar to raw_cpu_add_return_4 (e.g. what we use in __preempt_count_sub_return) but that can only conditionally add if the thing we're adding to has a certain value?

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 18:21):

in particular - a "subtract N from M but only if M is non-zero, then return M"

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 18:22):

you can, but it's not free

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 18:22):

i.e. you need to pay the cost of compare and jump

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 18:23):

it's not very different than this_cpu_read() and this_cpu_add()

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 18:29):

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 19:36):

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 19:38):

Lyude said:

also i just realized - do we want this to extend to raw_local_irq_enable()/raw_local_irq_disable()?

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 19:44):

but that's not urgent in my opinion.

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 19:47):

well in positive news I'm pretty sure I at least see why init/main.c can't be converted quite yet: we disable interrupts in start_kernel() before we call setup_per_cpu_areas() so presumably we just don't have access to local_interrupt_disable_state there

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 19:50):

I see, so we could replace that with a local_irq_restore() there.

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 19:51):

Or we keep local_irq_disable() logic there, but name it something else.

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 19:53):

Could you try if you 1) define local_irq_{enable,disable}() as local_interrupt_{enable,disable}() and 2) use local_irq_restore() and local_irq_restore() in start_kernel() and see if that would work?

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 19:54):

sure, though - I think the other spot that's going to be broken is the softirq handler iirc

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 19:54):

also - we should probably move local_interrupt_disable out of spinlock.h

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 19:55):

Lyude said:

sure, though - I think the other spot that's going to be broken is the softirq handler iirc

Because of the similar issue? Or a different issue?

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 19:57):

It's a different issue but I don't know what yet because I don't really have any knowledge of how this part of the kernel works (like, I have no idea how softirqs are handled in relation to the scheduler and normal interrupt handling and they seem to be intertwined in quite a number of spots).
My assumption is that we're starting with interrupts disabled from a context that's not getting converted.
gimme a bit, going to try to move the code around and actually try moving over local_irq_disable()/local_irq_enable() again

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 19:57):

which I think is probably not a bad idea because I'm not really sure how much I trust coccinelle not to randomly miss things

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 20:50):

BTW - still not getting a boot after fixing init/main.c, going to see if thsi is from the softirq stuff now.
Does anyone have any tricks for something I could do when the kernel hangs during booting? would be nice if I could know where it's stopping, but I assume it's much earlier during boot then I've ever debugged before

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 21:15):

wait. I have a really strong guess as to what's happening now.
So - if we're using irqsave/restore APIs on top of local_interrupt_enable()/disable() things always work fine, which we know because of the coccinelle patches I've written. Specifically, disable()/enable() doesn't work nor do unbalanced enables or disables.
I'm realizing it may entirely be because local_interrupt_enable() just doesn't work the other way around. Like - if we start with interrupts disabled through local_irq_save(flags) it's both not going to have a refcount that would allow it to enable interrupts since we've never disabled them through local_interrupt_disable(). And even if it did, it would still just try to restore a blank set of irq flags

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 21:16):

You mean unpaired local_irq_disable() and local_irq_enable()?

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 21:17):

It's dawning on me I'm not actually sure if we could convert these things without doing both APIs at the same time, and having one special API for init/main.c. or at the very least, trying to ensure that we never have a local_interrupt_enable() call in any kind of context where local_interrupt_disable() has not been called.

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 21:18):

Boqun Feng said:

You mean unpaired local_irq_disable() and local_irq_enable()?

yes. Though - keep in mind this was just me trying to apply one patch to convert the whole kernel, and noticing it didn't work unless I specifically used:

@depends on patch && !report@
@@
- local_irq_disable();
+ local_interrupt_disable();
...
- local_irq_enable();
+ local_interrupt_enable();

I did actually write an additional rule at some point to try converting single-use cases of enable/disable in a single function but it broke as well

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 21:18):

why do we need a special API for init/main.c? We can just use local_irq_save(flags) and then later on, enable it via local_irq_restore(flags)

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 21:18):

Boqun Feng said:

why do we need a special API for init/main.c? We can just use local_irq_save(flags) and then later on, enable it via local_irq_restore(flags)

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 21:43):

Boqun Feng said:

My guess: it has to do with the local_irq_disable() in __irq_exit_rcu().

https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/blob/8de13a2fdfa80a70e8e123a5f744b3b49aaa3971/kernel/softirq.c#L676

^ here on x86, irq is re-disabled when in __irq_exit_rcu() before going into softirq handling.

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 21:44):

it's special because I guess apic_eoi() will automatically enable the irq for the CPU on x86.

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 21:45):

eoi is the CPU saying the irq has been handled, and I think different archs have different strategies about whether that also enables interrupts. For example, I think on arm64, the interrupts are still disabled.

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 21:50):

so https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/blob/rust/irq-spinlock-convert-bh-special/kernel/softirq.c?ref_type=heads#L676

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 21:52):

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 22:04):

Ok, iret would just restore the previous EFLAGS

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 22:06):

So that's the corresponding irq enabling for the irq_disable() in __irq_exit_rcu()

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 22:07):

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 22:07):

Therefore pretend maintaining the refcount won't work I guess.

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 22:07):

an x86 instruction

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 22:14):

...also, where exactly does iret get called?

Lyude (Jul 29 2025 at 22:15):

is it just something auto-generated and added to the end of sysvec_posted_msi_notification()?

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 22:17):

restore_regs_and_return_to_kernel in arch/x86/entry/entry_64.S.

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 23:24):

OK, let's take common_interrupt as an example.

First in arch/x86/include/asm/idtentry.h, the DECLARE_IDTENTRY_IRQ() macro would call the assembler macro idtentry_irq defined in arch/x86/entry/entry_64.S. And eventually, it will generate an interrupt function asm_common_interrupt. In the generated asm_common_interrupt you can find it eventually call error_return (defined in entry_64.S as well):

asm_common_interrupt:
    ...
    call common_interrupt
    jump error_return

error_return will eventually execute iret.

Now the common_interrupt() function is more related the softirq issue, it's defined in arch/x86/kernel/irq.c by DEFINE_IDTENTRY_IRQ() macro (defined in idtentry.h as well). In most cases, it'll add irq_enter_rcu() and irq_exit_rcu() around the real interrupt handler (__common_interrupt()).

So over all, in a call trace that irq_enter_rcu() and irq_exit_rcu() are involved. The interrupt disabling/enabling are roughly as follow:

// interrupt gate automatically disables interrupts
__common_interrupt():
    common_interrupt():
        irq_enter_rcu();
        __common_interrupt():
            call_irq_handler();
            apic_eoi(); // <- may be called if call_irq_handler() fails
        irq_exit_rcu():
            __irq_exit_rcu():
                 local_irq_disable();
                 // ^ if irq is already disabled, it's fine, since we want to disable
                 // it anyway.
                 invoke_softirq():
                     __do_softirq():
                         handle_softirq():
                             local_irq_enable();
                             // ^ softirq should run with irq enabled,
                             // that's the point
                             <run softirq handlers>
                             local_irq_disable();
    error_return():
        iret // <- Re-enable the irq

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 23:25):

So semantically speaking, the local_irq_disable() in __irq_exit_rcu() should not participate the refcounting.

Boqun Feng (Jul 29 2025 at 23:33):

I.e. if we were to define local_irq_disable() as local_interrupt_disable(), we should replace that with a (unpaired) local_irq_save(flags).

Joel Fernandes (Jul 30 2025 at 07:24):

I have a feeling it might be better to just keep local_irq_disable() separate from the refcounted api mechanism that rust needs, and not convert it. IOW, I wonder if its really worth the complexity just to reduce the number of APIs.. If we want to unconditionally disable / enable IRQs, we shouldn't pretend to try to track it (especially in already working C code..)

Joel Fernandes (Jul 30 2025 at 07:32):

And we can sight the softirq case if we have to reason with Thomas and Peter about it..

Joel Fernandes (Jul 30 2025 at 07:34):

Thoughts?

Boqun Feng (Jul 30 2025 at 15:05):

I don't disagree a third set of APIs would work, but I feel like we haven't tried hard enough yet. The softirq thing is not really hard to resolve imo.

Joel Fernandes (Jul 30 2025 at 15:08):

Did we finish researching how many such "Uconditionally disable/enable IRQs" cases there? ISTR the idle loop does this too.

Boqun Feng (Jul 30 2025 at 15:08):

I don't think so

Joel Fernandes (Jul 30 2025 at 15:09):

ah ok

Boqun Feng (Jul 30 2025 at 15:09):

But probably idle and interrupt are all the cases

Boqun Feng (Jul 30 2025 at 15:12):

Give me a few days, let me figure out the list of unpaired local_irq_{enable,disable}.

Joel Fernandes (Jul 30 2025 at 16:12):

FWIW, I double checked and this seems sound from a "counter overwrite" nesting perspective. So we should be good.

irq_exit_rcu() ...
-               __preempt_count_add(NMI_OFFSET + HARDIRQ_OFFSET);       \
+               this_cpu_inc(nmi_nesting);                      \
+               __preempt_count_add(HARDIRQ_OFFSET);            \
+               if (!in_mni())                                  \
+                       __preempt_count_add(NMI_OFFSET);        \

The case I was worried about was, what if we get interrupted before the update to nmi_nesting got written by the outer NMI. But that should not matter as long as we don't call schedule() if preempt count is 0 from the inner-NMI on nmi_nesting == 0. Which would be a disaster to do any way...

The problem with the RCU dynticks counter is slightly different, if we clobber the RCU nesting counters and wrongly detect that we are in an outer NMI when in fact we are not, and the outer NMI happen to be in an RCu read-side section when the inner NMI came, we are screwed.. so that kind of makes sense there. Over here there is no such problem AFAICS.

Lyude (Jul 30 2025 at 16:22):

Boqun Feng said:

Give me a few days, let me figure out the list of unpaired local_irq_{enable,disable}.

Oh I have a coccinelle script for this

Lyude (Jul 30 2025 at 16:23):

Assuming you just mean functions only calling one or the other?

Boqun Feng (Jul 30 2025 at 16:28):

Lyude said:

Assuming you just mean functions only calling one or the other?

Not just that, sometimes one function only calls one because it's expecting another function call the other, and that's not the "unpaired" that would bother us.

Lyude (Jul 30 2025 at 16:58):

ah gotcha
