[    0.098235] hardirqs last enabled at (192536): __console_unlock (/home/lyudess/Projects/linux/worktrees/rust-upstreaming/./arch/x86/include/asm/irqflags.h:42 /home/lyudess/Projects/linux/worktrees/rust-upstreaming/./arch/x86/include/asm/irqflags.h:119 /home/lyudess/Projects/linux/worktrees/rust-upstreaming/./arch/x86/include/asm/irqflags.h:159 /home/lyudess/Projects/linux/worktrees/rust-upstreaming/kernel/printk/printk.c:344 /home/lyudess/Projects/linux/worktrees/rust-upstreaming/kernel/printk/printk.c:2885)
[    0.098235] hardirqs last disabled at (192541): __console_unlock (/home/lyudess/Projects/linux/worktrees/rust-upstreaming/kernel/printk/printk.c:342 /home/lyudess/Projects/linux/worktrees/rust-upstreaming/kernel/printk/printk.c:2885)
[    0.098235] softirqs last enabled at (0): 0x0
[    0.098235] softirqs last disabled at (192342): kernel_fpu_begin_mask (/home/lyudess/Projects/linux/worktrees/rust-upstreaming/./include/linux/bottom_half.h:20 /home/lyudess/Projects/linux/worktrees/rust-upstreaming/./arch/x86/include/asm/fpu/api.h:69 /home/lyudess/Projects/linux/worktrees/rust-upstreaming/arch/x86/kernel/fpu/core.c:445)

found one :)

Boqun Feng (Jul 30 2025 at 21:10):

Could you explain why?

Boqun Feng (Jul 30 2025 at 21:11):

Also share the code if you can, I assume you did the mapping local_irq_disable() -> local_interrupt_disable(), right?

Lyude (Jul 30 2025 at 21:20):

@Boqun Feng can't explain yet unfortunately, I'm about to head out to do an errand and found this before leaving. But I'm realizing I should actually verify that the check I came up with for this does what I expected winceI basically just added some code to check if interrupts were enabled at the end of local_interrupts_enable.
And no, in this branch I only have softirq.c converted over

Boqun Feng (Jul 30 2025 at 21:21):

Ok, no worries. I would probably do some investigation myself in the rest of the week.

Lyude (Aug 06 2025 at 17:45):

Boqun Feng said:

Ok, no worries. I would probably do some investigation myself in the rest of the week.

btw - any chance you got any time for this?

Boqun Feng (Aug 06 2025 at 17:45):

I am working on this actually, however I am yet unable to get anything meaningful update at the moment

Boqun Feng (Aug 06 2025 at 17:46):

will keep you posted

Lyude (Aug 06 2025 at 17:46):

sure thing. If it helps at all btw: I had the idea of doing something similar to what the kernel currently does with backtraces, where we can figure out a way to save a stackdepot backtrace for the last enable/disable call and then dump it to the kernel log once we get an imbalance

Boqun Feng (Aug 06 2025 at 17:50):

Yeah, that sounds reasonable to me.

Boqun Feng (Aug 06 2025 at 17:52):

However a bit tricky part is the kernel print code path may also using local_irq_disable() hence I'm trying to find a more reliable to output some information when an unbalance occurs.

Lyude (Aug 06 2025 at 17:52):

that explains a lot

Lyude (Aug 06 2025 at 17:54):

wonder if maybe we could have __local_irq_disable(bool save_trace), redefine local_irq_disable() as __local_irq_disable(true) and then just use __local_irq_disable(false) in the spots that we don't want to log

Boqun Feng (Aug 06 2025 at 17:56):

Not a bad idea, but we could just use raw_local_irq_disable() for that.

Lyude (Aug 06 2025 at 19:40):

@Benno Lossin I just noticed your comment about moving away from the generic lock framework, is there anything I should be keeping in mind for the rust side of the SpinlockIrq work?

Benno Lossin (Aug 06 2025 at 19:41):

if you think it's going to be easier to make a new type rather than a new backend, then I would do that

Benno Lossin (Aug 06 2025 at 19:41):

otherwise I think it's fine to keep it as-is

Boqun Feng (Aug 06 2025 at 19:42):

We should keep it as it is for now

Benno Lossin (Aug 06 2025 at 19:42):

we can change it at the same time as the normal spinlock

Lyude (Aug 06 2025 at 19:42):

TBH I don't think so - as it is I think what we've got with Backend is good, and will work when we someday add future spinlock variants (like SpinlockIrqRaw)

Benno Lossin (Aug 06 2025 at 19:43):

maybe we shouldn't remove the backend after all...

Benno Lossin (Aug 06 2025 at 19:43):

I think it was originally proposed by @Alice Ryhl and I agreed in the case of wwmutex, but now I'm not so sure in general

Boqun Feng (Aug 06 2025 at 19:44):

Yeah, it's more like something we don't necessarily to make it generic, but we don't any urgent reason to do the work.

Lyude (Aug 06 2025 at 19:44):

honestly I don't really see any issues with Backend as it is. I think it's actually quite a nice abstraction, even for ctx-free ww mutex stuff. Especially since it simplifies having global locks

Lyude (Aug 06 2025 at 19:45):

but that's just my 5¢ :)

Joel Fernandes (Aug 07 2025 at 21:56):

Hi, I worked on the top 2 commits here today: https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/jfern/linux.git/commit/?h=rust-spinlock-irq&id=5b23adbb23dac965774935c047cc510bfaaa91c5
(any feedback is welcomed)

It builds, will be doing further tests (But I'm out of office next week and slow the week after). But I'll be continuing to work on this!

When are you guys planning the next posting?

Joel Fernandes (Aug 07 2025 at 22:14):

Initial testing with perf for NMI testing looks good. I'll further stress it with rcutorture / locktorture.

Boqun Feng (Aug 08 2025 at 13:58):

Joel Fernandes (Aug 08 2025 at 13:59):

Ok and feel free to cherry pick the above two if they look good to you. I did multiple tests and it’s looking good on my end.

Joel Fernandes (Aug 08 2025 at 13:59):

The main notable change is I dropped the lock prefix for inc since I don’t think it’s required for nmi.

Lyude (Aug 28 2025 at 22:21):

https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/commits/rust/irq-spinlock

Lyude (Aug 28 2025 at 22:22):

added them at the bottom below all of the other patches

Boqun Feng (Aug 28 2025 at 22:24):

Quick update: my investigation showed me that one of the culprits is the raw_spin_rq_unlock_irq(), now I'm trying to figure out why..

Boqun Feng (Aug 28 2025 at 22:24):

The investigation was interrupted, but now I'm back on it.

Boqun Feng (Sep 02 2025 at 05:22):

Boqun Feng said:

Quick update: my investigation showed me that one of the culprits is the raw_spin_rq_unlock_irq(), now I'm trying to figure out why..

OK, this has to do with: 1) I'm using arm64 for investigation, and on arm64, preempt_count is per-thread and 2) in context switch you generally disable interrupts from one task and restore it at the other task.

So to make it work, we need to change arm64 context switch code :(

Boqun Feng (Sep 02 2025 at 05:24):

oh, or just arch_local_irq_disable() and arch_local_irq_enable() for context switch cases.

Boqun Feng (Sep 02 2025 at 06:00):

Lovely, we have code:

__pm_runtime_resume():
  spin_lock_irqsave();
  rpm_resume():
    rpm_callback():
      __rpm_callback():
        spin_unlock_irq();
        spin_lock_irq();
  spin_lock_irqrestore();

Lyude (Sep 17 2025 at 20:22):

BTW - going to try to send out a new version of this series this week or next

Lyude (Oct 09 2025 at 18:13):

ok - sorry it took me so long! I'm currently working on this now :)

Lyude (Oct 09 2025 at 18:14):

@Boqun Feng am I right in assuming we probably want to drop "locking: Switch to _irq_{disable,enable}() variants in cleanup guards" for the time being since we're limiting this API to being rust-only for now?

Boqun Feng (Oct 09 2025 at 18:15):

I don't think so

Boqun Feng (Oct 09 2025 at 18:15):

So first, it's a wrong signal to say the C API is Rust only.

Boqun Feng (Oct 09 2025 at 18:16):

The reason that we don't convert/converge APIs is because that's going to be a long term work. Not because the new API is rust-only

Boqun Feng (Oct 09 2025 at 18:17):

Second, the cleanup guard usage is actually correct as they are.

Boqun Feng (Oct 09 2025 at 18:23):

@Lyude I don't know where you got the impression that the API is rust-only, it's quite the opposite: we want to encourage people to use this API, so the conversion will be done naturally, i.e. new users should probably use this API even in C.

Lyude (Oct 09 2025 at 19:07):

gotcha

Lyude (Oct 09 2025 at 19:08):

yeah sorry! it's been a while so I definitely misremembered a few things

Boqun Feng (Oct 09 2025 at 19:08):

no worries

Lyude (Oct 09 2025 at 20:52):

Going back through everything here and making sure I've got the remaining todo items correct along with their resolutions:

  • NMI nesting - this is definitely solved
  • Understanding the performance impact - we know from @Joel Fernandes 's testing that it did seem like it added some time to interrupts being enabled/disabled, but that the time seemed negligable in comparison to the bigger bottleneck - lock contention
  • Have a plan for merging the 3 APIs into 2 - we have the evidence from trying to convert it so far that actually doing such a conversion is going to require figuring out some of the gotchas around things like arm64's context switching code.... oh and it looks like we actually solved it?

Lyude (Oct 09 2025 at 20:54):

I can see we figured out the arm64 issue but I don't think I see any explanation for the issue I brought up with the unrefcounted interrupt state changes w/r/t softirq.c

Lyude (Oct 09 2025 at 20:55):

yeah ok - and we're deciding not to handle all of that now because getting this working is going to be longterm work as a result of these issues

Boqun Feng (Oct 09 2025 at 20:55):

For "Have a plan for merging the 3 APIs into 2", my current thought is: it's in theorically doable, and we find a few things that we could make it work, but each of them requires some more changes, and we need to proceed with caution. Given the irq-disabling is important for real world drivers, it makes sense that we have 3 sets of API in the beginning, and consolidate in the future.

Lyude (Oct 09 2025 at 20:55):

so then the resolution for that should just be "Here's the issues we found, they can be fixed but doing so across the kernel is going to be a long term TODO" then correct?

Lyude (Oct 09 2025 at 20:56):

ah - ok, gotcha.

Boqun Feng (Oct 09 2025 at 20:56):

Lyude said:

so then the resolution for that should just be "Here's the issues we found, they can be fixed but doing so across the kernel is going to be a long term TODO" then correct?

Yeah, this is pretty much it.

Lyude (Oct 09 2025 at 20:56):

cool - and @Joel Fernandes just to double check, we're submitting your NMI nesting work as part of this series and not separately, correct?

Lyude (Oct 09 2025 at 20:56):

(I assume so since that makes the most sense, but figured I'd check in case you started trying to upstream in parallel)

Joel Fernandes (Oct 09 2025 at 20:56):

yes! Will be part of this series, not separate.

Lyude (Oct 09 2025 at 20:57):

cool :)

Lyude (Oct 09 2025 at 20:58):

ok - I should be able to send this asap then. it looks like we were lucky too and rebasing this required 0 work

Lyude (Oct 10 2025 at 21:44):

@Boqun Feng you still around? I should have a blurb for the cover letter written in just a moment if you want to take a look before I send it out, since it goes a bit into the issues that we ran into trying to convert the kernel

Lyude (Oct 10 2025 at 21:44):

figured I might as well save a round trip before sending it out

Looks like that rc1 has gotten pulled into rust-next, I just finished rebasing my patches so I'm going to send this series out now

Lyude (Oct 13 2025 at 15:47):

Boqun Feng (Oct 13 2025 at 15:48):

Yeah, I was about to say that the cover letter looks good to me

Boqun Feng (Oct 13 2025 at 15:49):

Lyude (Oct 13 2025 at 16:14):

I just realized - it seems like (at least if I'm guessing correctly) https://lkml.org/lkml/2025/10/13/1114 contains some unrelated detritus that I didn't notice, presumably because at some point I pulled a branch and did a rebase where I ran rustfmt on every commit and picked up a formatting error from someone else into Joel's patch. Do we want to resend or just wait for more comments?

Joel Fernandes (Oct 13 2025 at 16:16):

Maybe a reply to the patch with a new patch for just that one could be sufficient (git send email has a in-reply-to option)

Joel Fernandes (Oct 13 2025 at 16:17):

That’s what I normally do or reply saying that you noticed this issue and that you will fix it up along with any other comments. If there is a note like that , the maintainer knows that this issue needs to be resolved and can fix it up themselves or let you know

Boqun Feng (Oct 13 2025 at 16:17):

Yeah, just as Joel suggested, leaving a comment is fine.

Lyude (Oct 13 2025 at 20:49):

@Joel Fernandes when writing up the NMI mask patch, did you try seeing if DECLARE_PER_CPU_CACHE_HOT worked for defining the mask? Peter pointed out that it's in a different cacheline, though honestly all of this has me wondering how performance critical checking the nmi nesting count even is…

Joel Fernandes (Oct 13 2025 at 21:01):

I haven't tried that, I guess it is perf critical for high prequency NMI usecases like Perf, but I am not sure how much.

Joel Fernandes (Oct 13 2025 at 21:02):

I will look into it and get back @Lyude

Joel Fernandes (Oct 13 2025 at 21:16):

@Lyude AFAIK, all the per-cpu hot variables have to fit in a single cache-line

Joel Fernandes (Oct 13 2025 at 21:16):

So I am not sure if moving nmi nesting will be a good use of that space, I think its already close to filling.

Joel Fernandes (Oct 13 2025 at 21:17):

NMIs are disruptive as it is, so I would not think it is that performance critical :)

Joel Fernandes (Oct 13 2025 at 21:17):

@Boqun Feng any thoughts?

Joel Fernandes (Oct 13 2025 at 21:27):

I replied to Peter suggesting this, lets see what he says :)

Joel Fernandes (Oct 13 2025 at 21:28):

on second look, I think there's still room in the pcpu cache hot line

Joel Fernandes (Oct 13 2025 at 21:28):

and we could always have more than 1 cache-hot line right? :P

Boqun Feng (Oct 13 2025 at 21:44):

I think your reply is what I would do, so let's wait for peterz

Lyude (Oct 21 2025 at 20:21):

So: I've been working on fixing a compilation issue that I noticed got posted from the linux kernel testing bot regarding a build failure on " irq & spin_lock: Add counted interrupt disabling/enabling" on the hexagon platform. The main issue seems to be that I need raw_smp_processor_id defined on that platform for raw_cpu_write() to work. The problem is headers in C are evil and I can't actually seem to find a way of doing #include <linux/smp.h> from include/linux/irqflags.h that fixes hexagon but doesn't break x86_64, since then https://paste.centos.org/view/0ed5d21b this happens

Lyude (Oct 21 2025 at 20:21):

Does anyone know if there's like, a guide or reference for how to actually do inclusions of these headers properly?

Lyude (Oct 21 2025 at 20:22):

I am assuming this is all probably terrible implicit ordering that's not at all documented but I figured I should at least check

Lyude (Oct 21 2025 at 20:24):

i'd try to find out where I can include boot_cpu_data but, that is also very unclear and seems to be spread across multiple different locations depending on which arch (as in, even within the arch specific folders the places I find boot_cpu_data defined are not consistent filename wise)

Joel Fernandes (Oct 22 2025 at 15:20):

Regarding the preempt count, Peter's suggestion to expand preempt_count to 64-bit seems infeasible to me, since it breaks the atomicity of the decrement-and-test operation. Plus, it seems to offer no benefit and only drawbacks afaics. My plan for next spin is to fix an issue in the patch 1. by unconditionally setting the NMI bit in preempt_count 2. Moving the per-cpu nmi counter to the per-cpu hot area. I'll provide a patch doing this to you guys tomorrow, but let me know if you any disagreement with my plan.

Lyude (Oct 22 2025 at 15:57):

That sounds fine to me

Lyude (Oct 22 2025 at 17:53):

FWIW: I'm still trying to figure out a good solution to the cyclic header dependency. Trying to do a hexagon-arch specific include to workaround this seems like kind of a fragile approach, so I am starting to think the best thing to do might be to just split out local_interrupt_enable()/local_interrupt_disable() from irqflags.h into their own header, and then include it in the various kernel headers as needed.

Joel Fernandes (Oct 22 2025 at 17:55):

Sure, that sounds good to me and also illustrates the problem if anything.

Lyude (Oct 22 2025 at 19:43):

ok - https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/commits/rust/irq-spinlock fixed the build error :)

Boqun Feng (Oct 29 2025 at 19:23):

@Lyude I have a request ;-) Could you make the Cc list the same for every patch in the next version, i.e. make sure everybody Cced receive the whole patchset? I know there are some subsystems that people try to avoid spamming, but in core kernel/locking, it's generally a good practice to Cc all patches. I would also like to get more Rust reviewers to take a look at C side changes as well. Thanks!

Lyude (Oct 29 2025 at 19:23):

You mean for the next version of SpinlockIrq? Sure, I can figure something out for that

Boqun Feng (Oct 29 2025 at 19:24):

Yes ;-)

Boqun Feng (Oct 29 2025 at 19:24):

Thank you!

and it changes the maximum of their (hardirq and nmi) nesting level.

Sounds fine to me

btw - did you manage to get a new version of the patch ready?

Joel Fernandes (Nov 12 2025 at 18:10):

Joel Fernandes (Nov 12 2025 at 18:10):

Lyude (Nov 12 2025 at 18:11):

no problem!

Joel Fernandes (Nov 12 2025 at 18:11):

I was just talking to Boqun About it today

Joel Fernandes (Nov 12 2025 at 18:11):

I can get it out to you by Friday if that’s OK

Joel Fernandes (Nov 12 2025 at 18:11):

I could post it to the list and you can pick it up from there?

Lyude (Nov 12 2025 at 18:11):

yeah that works fine for me! and yeah that sgtm

Joel Fernandes (Nov 14 2025 at 20:23):

@Lyude here is the latest patch [1]. I tested on my side, but could you cherry-pick it with your series and report back if it looks good?
[1] https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/jfern/linux.git/commit/?h=nmi-counter-11-14-2025&id=86448edac31e2b5cab2200c80eaace1e633f2d55

I think there is no urgency from my side with posting it, so I'd rather wait for your results as well and/or let you carry it if its Ok. From my side, my testing looks good. Also, I squashed the 2 patches into one because there is a circular dependency otherwise.

Joel Fernandes (Nov 14 2025 at 20:24):

The only change I made since last revision is to unconditionally set the NMI_MASK on NMI entry:

+       __preempt_count_add(HARDIRQ_OFFSET);        \
+       preempt_count_set(preempt_count() | NMI_MASK);  \

Lyude (Nov 17 2025 at 22:00):

Joel Fernandes said:

Lyude here is the latest patch [1]. I tested on my side, but could you cherry-pick it with your series and report back if it looks good?
[1] https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/jfern/linux.git/commit/?h=nmi-counter-11-14-2025&id=86448edac31e2b5cab2200c80eaace1e633f2d55

I think there is no urgency from my side with posting it, so I'd rather wait for your results as well and/or let you carry it if its Ok. From my side, my testing looks good. Also, I squashed the 2 patches into one because there is a circular dependency otherwise.

np - I'll take a look next chance I get. FWIW, there's not too much to test beyond just making sure things boot, maybe running kunit just to make sure things look good

Lyude (Nov 17 2025 at 22:14):

@Joel Fernandes what commit is this patch based on top of? it doesn't seem to go at the start of my series, and the branch I posted last time has the two NMI bits at the very start of the series

Lyude (Nov 17 2025 at 22:17):

ok - i see, forgot I can just fetch the commit and go from there. i'll move it to where it probably should be.

Lyude (Nov 17 2025 at 23:26):

ok - https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/commits/rust/irq-spinlock should be rebased now, I'm probably going to go through and apply any missing R-B tags tomorrow and send out the new version of the patch series then

Joel Fernandes (Nov 18 2025 at 19:51):

Ok thanks @Lyude !

Joel Fernandes (Nov 18 2025 at 19:53):

checked and it lgtm.

Lyude (Nov 18 2025 at 22:24):

cool - this might take me till tomorrow because I realized there's still some small review comments I need to address. Got myself too distracted trying to diagnose runtime PM issues with my laptop's discrete GPU :S

Joel Fernandes (Nov 18 2025 at 22:27):

Heh

Lyude (Nov 19 2025 at 22:01):

Just noticed - we're getting asked for a benchmark regarding the BackendInContext stuff now. I assume it does make things faster considering I kind of doubt we'd have multiple variants of any of the irq spinlocks in the kernel in the first place, but I'll definitely need to figure this out before sending another respin

Lyude (Nov 19 2025 at 22:05):

actually, we might already have a response for this

Lyude (Nov 19 2025 at 22:08):

@Andreas Hindborg ^ figured I should probably ask: while I don't currently have a benchmark that explicitly checks the performance gain from BackendInContext, I do actually know that we do see measurable performance differences between refcounted IRQ enable/disable (slower) vs. non-refcounted IRQ enable/disable, and IIRC the bottleneck is the conditional that we had to add for checking the interrupt refcount.
With this being said though, since lock_with() would avoid touching the interrupt refcount - I think it's very likely we'd see pretty much the same performance difference between lock() (slower) vs. lock_with() (faster) since the latter skips the conditional that caused the earlier performance hit.
Does that seem like enough proof, or would you rather I still write up a benchmark?

Boqun Feng (Nov 19 2025 at 22:27):

Notice that in an interrupt handler, where interrupts are disabled (because Linux dosen't support nested interrupts), the refcount could still be 0, so if we still use SpinLockIrq::lock(), it'll go through the interrupt disabling logic and issue the actual irq disable instructions. lock_with() can save the cost of that.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 08:35):

For non-interrupt context, I do not believe this code is going to make a difference. You are incrementing/decrementing a refcount in a cache line that the CPU already has exclusive ownership of.

For interrupt context, I am not aware of the cost of disabling interrupts by writing the interrupt control register, if interrupts are already disabled. But I would assume it is negligible.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 08:37):

Which is why I ask you to measure this. It should be quick enough to do a micro benchmark, but better yet to use this in a real example. If you want, I can put it into the nvme driver. I think there is a place for this patch in there.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 08:38):

It's just a lot of added complexity for something that probably does not make a difference.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 08:40):

Actually, I would suggest the following:

  • Drop the major part of the series with the BackendInContext etc.
  • Build your driver with the first part of the series.
  • Apply the second part of the series locally and adopt your driver to use it.
  • Benchmark the two versions against each other.
  • If you see a difference, send the rest of the series, with your results.

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 14:08):

I don’t think you need to drop the series to for benchmark purpose, having it does affect how lock() works.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:09):

I'm not saying you need to drop the entire series, but I would suggest holding off on the last part.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:10):

Because I don't think it provides any benefit in practice.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:10):

I don't understand why you would want to introduce such complexity without having tangible measurements that it is actually beneficial.

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 14:11):

Well, we do have benchmarks of the performance of the patch series is one reason

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 14:11):

E.g. @Joel Fernandes 's refscale test

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 14:12):

Or whatever the test is called, sorry it's been a bit since I looked at it

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:12):

That's cool, I would suggest sharing those numbers so we can have a more informed discussion.

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 14:13):

Yeah sure thing, they should be in the conversation history here but I'll find the exact posts when I sit in front of my computer

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 14:13):

Probably would help for me to make sure they stay in the cover letter for future patch series

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 14:14):

Fwiw too: I don't think it's interrupt disabling that is the bottleneck, it's the conditional of checking the interrupt disable counter

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:15):

I am genuinely curious, because I cannot wrap my head around a single integer increment/decrement of a cpu local cache line making any kind of practical difference.

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 14:16):

Yeah it was a bit surprising to me as well tbh

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 14:18):

Andreas Hindborg said:

I am genuinely curious, because I cannot wrap my head around a single integer increment/decrement of a cpu local cache line making any kind of practical difference.

but what about the actual interrupt disabling instructions?

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 14:19):

in the irq context.

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 14:20):

What you are asking is basically limiting the Rust api from the equal ability as C API.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:21):

Boqun Feng said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

I am genuinely curious, because I cannot wrap my head around a single integer increment/decrement of a cpu local cache line making any kind of practical difference.

but what about the actual interrupt disabling instructions?

As I said above somewhere, I do not know. It could be, but it would surprise me. It will be architecture specific, but I would be surprised if it has a high cost to write the interrupt control bit with the same value that it already is.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:38):

Boqun Feng said:

What you are asking is basically limiting the Rust api from the equal ability as C API.

We don't have to do everything the way C does it. If it complicates the codebase, we should only do it if it has practical implications. Not just because we can.

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 14:39):

Andreas Hindborg said:

I'm not saying you need to drop the entire series, but I would suggest holding off on the last part.

What I wanted to say is you don't have to drop the last part to test for performance.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:41):

Boqun Feng said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

I'm not saying you need to drop the entire series, but I would suggest holding off on the last part.

What I wanted to say is you don't have to drop the last part to test for performance.

Right. But let's not merge it if there is no practical benefit.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:42):

Not sure if we are on the same page? :)

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 14:42):

Andreas Hindborg said:

Boqun Feng said:

What you are asking is basically limiting the Rust api from the equal ability as C API.

We don't have to do everything the way C does it. If it complicates the codebase, we should only do it if it has practical implications. Not just because we can.

I'm talking about the same ability or (express ability) as C.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:48):

Boqun Feng said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Boqun Feng said:

What you are asking is basically limiting the Rust api from the equal ability as C API.

We don't have to do everything the way C does it. If it complicates the codebase, we should only do it if it has practical implications. Not just because we can.

I'm talking about the same ability or (express ability) as C.

I understand. What I am trying to express is that I do not think we always need to have exactly the same express ability as C, if it comes at the cost of high complexity API implementation, as it does in this case. Especially if it does not make a difference in practice. However, if it does make a difference in practice, I do believe it is worth the cost of the more complex code. But until someone measures the actual impact of this extra express ability in practice, for this particular API, we do not know if it makes a difference.

My gut feeling is, it is not going to make a difference in this case. I could be wrong though.

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 14:52):

rust_kernel: lock() when irq is disabled, 100 times cost Delta { nanos: 375 }
rust_kernel: lock_with() when irq is disabled, 100 times cost Delta { nanos: 208 }
rust_kernel: lock() when irq is enabled, 100 times cost Delta { nanos: 708 }

on my M4 system

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 14:53):

diff --git a/rust/kernel/sync/lock/spinlock.rs b/rust/kernel/sync/lock/spinlock.rs
index 81384ea23995..ed056d701758 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/sync/lock/spinlock.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/sync/lock/spinlock.rs
@@ -411,3 +411,91 @@ fn spinlock_irq_condvar() -> Result {
         Ok(())
     }
 }
+
+#[kunit_tests(rust_spinlock_performance)]
+mod performance {
+    use super::*;
+    #[test]
+    fn spinlock_lock_with_vs_lock() -> Result {
+        use crate::workqueue::*;
+        use crate::interrupt::*;
+        use crate::sync::Arc;
+        use crate::time::{Instant, Monotonic};
+
+        use crate::sync::completion::Completion;
+
+        let comp1 = Arc::pin_init(Completion::new(), GFP_KERNEL)?;
+        let comp2= Arc::pin_init(Completion::new(), GFP_KERNEL)?;
+        let comp3 = Arc::pin_init(Completion::new(), GFP_KERNEL)?;
+
+        let lock = Arc::pin_init(new_spinlock_irq!(()), GFP_KERNEL)?;
+
+        system().try_spawn(
+            GFP_KERNEL,
+            {
+                let comp = comp1.clone();
+                let lock = lock.clone();
+                move || {
+                    let disable = local_interrupt_disable();
+                    let instant = Instant::<Monotonic>::now();
+
+                    for _ in 0..100 {
+                        let _  = lock.lock();
+                    }
+                    let d = instant.elapsed();
+                    pr_info!("lock() when irq is disabled, 100 times cost {:?}\n", d);
+                    drop(disable);
+                    comp.complete_all();
+                }
+            }
+        )?;
+
+        comp1.wait_for_completion();
+
+        system().try_spawn(
+            GFP_KERNEL,
+            {
+                let comp = comp2.clone();
+                let lock = lock.clone();
+                move || {
+                    let disable = local_interrupt_disable();
+                    let instant = Instant::<Monotonic>::now();
+
+                    for _ in 0..100 {
+                        let _  = lock.lock_with(&disable);
+                    }
+
+                    let d = instant.elapsed();
+                    pr_info!("lock_with() when irq is disabled, 100 times cost {:?}\n", d);
+                    drop(disable);
+                    comp.complete_all();
+                }
+            }
+        )?;
+
+        comp2.wait_for_completion();
+
+        system().try_spawn(
+            GFP_KERNEL,
+            {
+                let comp = comp3.clone();
+                let lock = lock.clone();
+                move || {
+                    let instant = Instant::<Monotonic>::now();
+
+                    for _ in 0..100 {
+                        let _  = lock.lock();
+                    }
+
+                    let d = instant.elapsed();
+                    pr_info!("lock() when irq is enabled, 100 times cost {:?}\n", d);
+                    comp.complete_all();
+                }
+            }
+        )?;
+
+        comp3.wait_for_completion();
+
+        Ok(())
+    }
+}

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:53):

Apple M4 running in a VM?

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 14:53):

@Lyude the diff is based on your latest https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/commits/rust/irq-spinlock

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 14:54):

Andreas Hindborg said:

Apple M4 running in a VM?

Yes.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:54):

Probably interrupt enable/disable requires hypervisor activation.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:54):

But that is a valid reason for optimizing anyway.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:55):

You get similar results when you run multiple times?

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 14:55):

Yeah, just giving an idea about how to profiling this.

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 14:57):

rust_kernel: lock() when irq is disabled, 100 times cost Delta { nanos: 500 }
rust_kernel: lock_with() when irq is disabled, 100 times cost Delta { nanos: 292 }
rust_kernel: lock() when irq is enabled, 100 times cost Delta { nanos: 834 }

rust_kernel: lock() when irq is disabled, 100 times cost Delta { nanos: 459 }
rust_kernel: lock_with() when irq is disabled, 100 times cost Delta { nanos: 291 }
rust_kernel: lock() when irq is enabled, 100 times cost Delta { nanos: 709 }

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:57):

I did not consider hypervisor activation.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 14:59):

OK, that is cool. Just put that in the commit message then :+1:

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 15:00):

Thank you, but appreciate if we can get more data @Lyude @Joel Fernandes feel free to test on the system you care about.

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 15:01):

I run it with the following command:

./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --make_options CLIPPY=1 --arch arm64 --kconfig_add CONFIG_RUST=y --make_options LLVM=1 --kconfig_add CONFIG_SMP=y --kconfig_add CONFIG_EXPERT=y --qemu_args "-smp 4" rust_spinlock_performance --raw_output

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 15:01):

you may need to change it --arch arm64 part

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 15:03):

It would also be nice to see what it means in a real workload.

Andreas Hindborg (Nov 20 2025 at 15:04):

We increase the duration of a 2ns operation to 4-5 ns. It might be lost in noise for real workloads.

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 15:04):

That's why I mentioned about keeping the patch as it is, and that allows people to use lock_with() in their code to see whether there is any benefit.

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 18:42):

@Boqun Feng @Andreas Hindborg ^ if you're curious, x86_64 on my i7-13700H under kvm

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 18:51):

also - I'll leave it out for now but did we want to actually include this benchmark in the patch series?

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 18:52):

I will say let's leave it out.

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 18:52):

It's a quick way to get some results, but ideally, we want something better.

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 18:53):

like cargo bench.

Joel Fernandes (Nov 20 2025 at 19:21):

I wonder if this test can be integrated into Refscale. The main addition afaics versus the existing Refscale test I had proposed, is, it is testing the overhead of calling the api when interrupts were already disabled, versus when it wasn’t already disable. Performance wise though, that would not be different than the existing local_irq_save. We could simply test local_irq_save in Refscale and compare it and then compare it with the new local_interrupt_disable when interrupts were already disabled. I suspect the delta will be in the few nano seconds range so maybe the testing wouldn’t buy us much but probably good thing to check time permitting.

Joel Fernandes (Nov 20 2025 at 19:23):

Oh, though the kunit test does also test the rust locking and spinlock layer.

Joel Fernandes (Nov 20 2025 at 19:25):

(Btw my internet is completely down today, I think construction nicked a fiber optic cable in the vicinity :joy:so I’ll be mostly offline today till I can get my hotspot situated).

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 19:31):

Huh. Well then, it actually seems like it might not always make a difference on all arches

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 19:31):

rust_kernel: lock() when irq is disabled, 100 times cost Delta { nanos: 853 }
rust_kernel: lock_with() when irq is disabled, 100 times cost Delta { nanos: 831 }
rust_kernel: lock() when irq is enabled, 100 times cost Delta { nanos: 1691 }

I had one a moment ago as well where lock() and lock_with() with IRQs disabled had identical values

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 20:27):

BTW - going to attempt at sending out a respin of this series in just a moment. I say 'attempt' since I just modified my cc_cmd script to go through, collect a full list of CCs for the whole patch series, and then paste those same CCs so they're included for each patch. this is like 60 email addresses though so there's a really good chance it's not actually going to send

Joel Fernandes (Nov 20 2025 at 20:28):

I follow a similar workflow and it does become challenging when you have patches touching too many different areas. Perhaps switching to running get_maintainer pl on a per patch basis is better for such cases.

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 20:29):

if it doesn't send - is there any way y'all trim down the cc list, preferably one that isn't manual? I know the big reason I didn't have my scrip-ok lol

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 20:29):

was just about to ask

Joel Fernandes (Nov 20 2025 at 20:29):

Passing get maintainer script to cc cmd is best

Joel Fernandes (Nov 20 2025 at 20:31):

I have this in my ~/.gitconfig which I uncomment, when I was to do trimmed cc-list on a per-patch basis:

# [sendemail]
#  tocmd ="`pwd`/scripts/get_maintainer.pl --nogit --nogit-fallback --norolestats --nol"
#  cccmd ="`pwd`/scripts/get_maintainer.pl --nogit --nogit-fallback --norolestats --nom"

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 20:32):

Some people prefer to receive the whole patch series though.

Joel Fernandes (Nov 20 2025 at 20:32):

Then git send-email will pick up the cc list that way.

Joel Fernandes (Nov 20 2025 at 20:33):

Some people prefer to receive the whole patch series though.

Yeah, I think those can be explicit CC: entries in every patch, I think. Or perhaps, more augmented --cc-cmd line (combine get_maintainer.pl with explicit entries)

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 20:33):

yeah but it doesn't give you the same cc for each patch by default which was the issue (hold on, if anyone is curious I'll post the script I use that fixes this in a sec)

Boqun Feng (Nov 20 2025 at 20:35):

I think at least Thomas Gleixner + all locking maintainers should be Cced for the whole series.

Joel Fernandes (Nov 20 2025 at 20:36):

I think git send-email permits multiple --cc= entries, even with the above snippet uncommented. So just do that for Thomas etc.

Joel Fernandes (Nov 20 2025 at 20:38):

--dry-run is your friend.

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 20:39):

https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/ml-cc-cmd

Lyude (Nov 20 2025 at 21:48):

ok new version is out

Lyude (Dec 10 2025 at 16:12):

JFYI: finally got a successful CKI build, will try to post a new version of the patch series today

Gary Guo (Dec 16 2025 at 12:35):

I wonder if the C patches should be taken first. I feel like the C patches are good enough while Rust ones may need a few more iterations

Lyude (Dec 16 2025 at 16:45):

@Gary Guo What makes you say that? We're already on v16, I think we've gone over the design of the rust side of this a couple of times now

Lyude (Dec 16 2025 at 16:47):

(also, I think boqun is already planning on picking this up for 7.0)

Gary Guo (Dec 16 2025 at 17:14):

I'm not currently sure about the Context design. Is there a link to point to where it's being discussed first? v16 makes it super difficult for me to find the discussions (especially there isn't a link to older versions in the cover letter, just to the last one)

(Also, there're a few issues with patches themselves not being split correctly, there're patches adding documentation for things introduced later and a patch including a todo!() even)

Gary Guo (Dec 16 2025 at 17:15):

Patch 1-9 + 17 looks fine to me, but the context thing is a bit werid

Lyude (Dec 16 2025 at 17:24):

Gary Guo said:

I'm not currently sure about the Context design. Is there a link to point to where it's being discussed first? v16 makes it super difficult for me to find the discussions (especially there isn't a link to older versions in the cover letter, just to the last one)

(Also, there're a few issues with patches themselves not being split correctly, there're patches adding documentation for things introduced later and a patch including a todo!() even)

I think most of the conversation around this happened both on zulip (see the beginning of this topic) and on some of the early versions of the patch series. The refcounting portions we picked up around v6, "rust: Add irq abstraction, SpinLockIrq" was the name of the patch series originally since we didn't originally have changes on the C side

Lyude (Dec 16 2025 at 17:25):

The main reason we want the context stuff is because there's a performance benefit to when we're able to skip doing refcounting for interrupts, and having a ZST type represent "IRQs are disabled while this value is in scope" makes it easy to implement that optimization

Lyude (Dec 16 2025 at 17:26):

Is there any other info you need me to dig up? it's a really big and long conversation so I don't blame you for having some trouble finding all of the info

Gary Guo (Dec 16 2025 at 17:37):

The refcounted design is good to me, and I get the reasoning behind wanting to use token types to avoid overhead. However I am not sure the way generics and assoc types being used here is correct (it's certainly confusing for the reader).

I'll do some poking to your code to see if I can come up with something cleaner.

Lyude (Dec 16 2025 at 17:37):

sgtm

Gary Guo (Dec 16 2025 at 18:17):

I think this is a cleaner design for the lock_with method:

unsafe trait LockWith<C> {
    type ActingBackend: Backend;
}

impl<T: ?Sized, B: Backend> Lock<T, B> {
    pub fn lock_with<'a, C>(&'a self, _: C) -> Guard<'a, T, B::ActingBackend>
    where
        B: LockWith<C>,
        C: 'a, // Lifetimes in the context must outlive the guard.
     { ... }
}

unsafe impl LockWith<&LocalInterruptDisabled> for SpinLockIrqBackend {
    type ActingBackend = SpinLockBackend;
}

although, I do wonder if this should actually be preferred vs just an inherent method on Lock<T, SpinLockIrqBackend>.

Benno Lossin (Dec 17 2025 at 03:27):

Didn't we want to change the generic lock API to having each lock on its own?

Boqun Feng (Dec 17 2025 at 05:46):

Gary Guo said:

I think this is a cleaner design for the lock_with method:

unsafe trait LockWith<C> {
    type ActingBackend: Backend;
}

impl<T: ?Sized, B: Backend> Lock<T, B> {
    pub fn lock_with<'a, C>(&'a self, _: C) -> Guard<'a, T, B::ActingBackend>
    where
        B: LockWith<C>,
        C: 'a, // Lifetimes in the context must outlive the guard.
     { ... }
}

unsafe impl LockWith<&LocalInterruptDisabled> for SpinLockIrqBackend {
    type ActingBackend = SpinLockBackend;
}

although, I do wonder if this should actually be preferred vs just an inherent method on Lock<T, SpinLockIrqBackend>.

I think inherent method on Lock<T, SpinLockIrqBackend> is probably better, we only have this type of usage here (for spinlockirq)

Lyude (Jan 15 2026 at 22:18):

Lyude (Jan 15 2026 at 22:21):

i was really under the impression this was going to be able to get in for 7.0 ._.

Lyude (Jan 15 2026 at 22:48):

if there is anything i can do to get this done on time, please let me know, or if we could get it in for rc3 or something. 7.0 has basically been on all of my status reports and standups and I really was not expecting this.

Lyude (Jan 15 2026 at 22:54):

in the mean time, when should I expect to need this ready for 7.1?

Lyude (Jan 15 2026 at 23:04):

also, sorry for all the questions - but @Boqun Feng what exactly is stopping any of the C portions from this patch series from being included? i don't see any pending reviews on that part of the series, and that seems to be what we would really want to make sure gets in for rc1

Lyude (Jan 15 2026 at 23:05):

is it at all possible for at least some of this to actually get in for 7.0?

Boqun Feng (Jan 15 2026 at 23:44):

Lyude said:

@Boqun Feng in the future if we're planning on getting something in for a release would you mind letting me know a deadline if that's ok to ask?

I can try to do a better job next time, but there are still a few comments unresolved for v16 since Dec 22, so I wasn't sure that you will have enough time to resolve them all or what's your plan. Given the nature of community, I tend to give people enough time to finish what they need to do.

But in general, I will send my PR to tip during -r3 to -r5 of the previous cycle, so for example if we want it to be in v7.1, we need to finalize it before -rc3 of v7.0.

Lyude (Jan 15 2026 at 23:56):

gotcha, thank you!

Boqun Feng (Jan 16 2026 at 00:02):

Lyude said:

what exactly is stopping any of the C portions from this patch series from being included?

I'm mostly waiting for some tags from Peter and others, but I guess I could proceed without them. Peter did left some comments at v13, and never responded to Joel's solution. It's not necessarily a blocker, but I would ping him as well.

Lyude (Jan 16 2026 at 00:18):

Boqun Feng said:

Lyude said:

@Boqun Feng in the future if we're planning on getting something in for a release would you mind letting me know a deadline if that's ok to ask?

I can try to do a better job next time, but there are still a few comments unresolved for v16 since Dec 22, so I wasn't sure that you will have enough time to resolve them all or what's your plan. Given the nature of community, I tend to give people enough time to finish what they need to do.

But in general, I will send my PR to tip during -r3 to -r5 of the previous cycle, so for example if we want it to be in v7.1, we need to finalize it before -rc3 of v7.0.

also np - honestly it's mostly on me because I also lost track of it with some of the stuff I had going on in december. Hopefully shouldn't happen again though now that i know the deadlines for the future :)

Lyude (Feb 03 2026 at 00:32):

Lyude (Feb 03 2026 at 22:23):

@Boqun Feng cool! think you could link to the branch that you pushed them to?

Boqun Feng (Feb 03 2026 at 22:24):

https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/boqun/linux.git/log/?h=rust-sync

Lyude (Feb 03 2026 at 22:25):

yayyyyyy

Lyude (Feb 04 2026 at 21:06):

@Gary Guo BTW - how exactly are we going to handle spinlockirq for GlobalGuard without having a trait for this behavior?

Lyude (Feb 04 2026 at 21:07):

I would think we still at least need to be able to add a generic to GlobalGuard to handle tracking what backend the returned lock is

Lyude (Feb 04 2026 at 21:10):

(I'm also still not entirely sure I understand why this is simpler even after reading back the convo here. It seems simpler in the immediate now, but:

  • raw IRQ spinlocks are going to eventually need to also have lock_with() implemented I would assume
  • we have both LockedBy and GlobalGuard that currently generalize over locks
  • aren't there other locks with context requirements (preempt?) in the kernel?

Gary Guo (Feb 04 2026 at 21:13):

Raw IRQ would need the same, yes, that can also be inherent impl. I don't think we have the need to be more generic contexts, at least for now.

Lyude (Feb 04 2026 at 21:14):

so are we just dropping globalguard support then?

Gary Guo (Feb 04 2026 at 21:17):

GlobalLock is possible with something like

struct GlobalSpinLockIrqBackendAdapter<T>(T);

impl<B: GlobalLockBackend<Backend = SpinLockIrqBackend>> GlobalLockBackend for GlobalSpinLockIrqBackendAdapter<B> {
    const NAME: &'static  CStr = B::NAME;
    type Item = B::Item;
    type Backend = SpinLockBackend;

    fn get_lock_class() -> Pin<&'static LockClassKey> {
        B::get_lock_class()
    }
}

impl<B: GlobalLockBackend<Backend = SpinLockIrqBackend>> for GlobalLock<B>{
    pub fn lock_with(self: &'static Self) -> GlobalGuard<GlobalSpinLockIrqBackendAdapter<B>> { ... }
}

Although I might avoid adding this if there's no need yet.

Gary Guo (Feb 04 2026 at 21:18):

BTW, global lock is also something that I am trying to get rid of -- it requires unsafe to initialize and does not drop the content on module unload. However I am still experimenting the exact replacement.

Lyude (Feb 04 2026 at 22:11):

gotcha

Lyude (Feb 04 2026 at 22:11):

that makes things easy then :)

Boqun Feng (Mar 13 2026 at 22:59):

@Lyude sorry for the late response. I don't think at this moment, there is anything needed from your side.

Though, I need to apply Peter's 64bit preempt_count changes, and I will have a more clear picture whether the whole thing can be in 7.1 or not by the end of next week. Worst case, we may defer it to 7.2.

Thank you for the hard work.

no problem at all, thank you for the help ^^

Boqun Feng (Mar 23 2026 at 15:18):

Lyude (Apr 27 2026 at 18:19):

Hey! Just checking in what the status is here

Boqun Feng (Apr 27 2026 at 18:25):

Got a bit distraction, but going to send out the final version at end of this week.

Alice Ryhl (May 05 2026 at 10:43):

I'm also curious what the status is.

Boqun Feng (May 05 2026 at 13:35):

Let's just say making preempt_count 64bit is not as simple as it looks like :(

Alice Ryhl (May 05 2026 at 13:48):

:(

Boqun Feng (May 05 2026 at 15:44):

Actually, I just realized we don't need to make preempt_count() return a long, because the effective bits can always fit in 32 bits.

Boqun Feng (May 05 2026 at 15:48):

This is the result of that observation: https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/boqun/linux.git/commit/?h=rust-sync&id=455c55fbe658c53f8b782f1ab5283cb43c06b50d

Gary Guo (May 05 2026 at 15:50):

Instead of PREEMPT_LONG, it might be better to use PREEMPT_64BIT

Boqun Feng (May 05 2026 at 15:51):

Right

Boqun Feng (May 06 2026 at 07:41):

I think I got it right in rust-sync, I will need to run some tests with NMI tomorrow and then send it out.

Boqun Feng (May 07 2026 at 04:43):

some updates: the test went well (I used perf to trigger NMI path just make sure that works), but I found some more tiny issues when PREEMPT_DEBUG=y. So some more changes and tests are needed.

Do you need anything from me btw?
